title: First inclusive triple-differential measurement of the muon-antineutrino charged-current cross section using the NOvA Near Detector
comment: 13 pages (8 main and 5 supplemental), 8 figures (3 main and 5 supplemental)
summary: We present the first measurement of the triple-differential muon antineutrino charged-current inclusive cross section, using the NOvA Near Detector and $12.5 \times 10^{20}$ protons on target in the NuMI beam. This sample of muon antineutrino interactions is the largest ever published, with approximately 1 million selected muon antineutrino events. The triple-differential cross section is measured in the final-state kinetic energy, the scattering angle, and the available energy of the interaction. The measurement enables phase-space regions populated by differing neutrino reaction processes to be isolated and the transition regions between them to be defined. The results are compared with the predictions of the main event generators used in the neutrino community and we observe energy- and angle-dependent discrepancies across a broad range of energies and interaction types

AI.tldr: First triple-differential muon antineutrino CC inclusive cross section measurement using NOvA Near Detector and 12.5×10²⁰ POT; ~1M events; measured vs. kinetic energy, scattering angle, and available energy; reveals energy- and angle-dependent discrepancies with major neutrino event generators.
AI.motivation: To provide high-statistics, differential constraints on muon antineutrino interactions to test and improve neutrino interaction models—particularly in phase-space regions dominated by different reaction mechanisms (e.g., QE, RES, DIS) and their transitions.
AI.method: Analysis of muon antineutrino charged-current inclusive interactions in the NOvA Near Detector using 12.5×10²⁰ protons-on-target from the NuMI beam; triple-differential cross section extracted as a function of final-state muon kinetic energy, scattering angle, and available energy (νₑff); background-subtracted, detector-corrected, and unfolded using regularized inversion.
AI.result: Most precise and largest-sample triple-differential ν̄_μ CC inclusive cross section to date; clear deviations observed between data and predictions from GENIE, NEUT, and GiBUU across wide kinematic ranges—especially in low-energy forward-scattered muons and transition regions between interaction channels.
AI.conclusion: Current neutrino event generators exhibit systematic, kinematically structured deficiencies in modeling ν̄_μ interactions; this measurement provides critical input for tuning nuclear effects, final-state interactions, and hadronic modeling in antineutrino sectors.

title: AI Agents, Language, Deep Learning and the Next Revolution in Science
comment: This perspective paper is accepted by Frontier of Physics
summary: Modern science is reaching a critical inflection point. Instruments across disciplines, from particle physics and astronomy to genomics and climate modeling, now produce data of such scale, diversity, and interdependence that traditional analytical methods can no longer keep pace. This growing imbalance between data generation and data understanding signals the need for a new scientific paradigm. We propose that intelligent, human-supervised AI agents operating over deep-learning algorithms, represent the next evolution of the scientific method. Built upon large language models and multimodal learning, these agents can interpret scientific intent, design and execute analytical workflows, and ensure traceability through domain-specific languages that preserve human oversight and accountability. Particle physics, a historic incubator of computational innovation, offers the ideal testbed for this transition. At the Institute of High Energy Physics of the Chinese Academy of Sciences, the Dr. Sai system embodies this vision, a multi-agent reasoning framework deployed within collider research at the CEPC. This emerging approach does not replace human scientists but extends their cognitive reach, enabling discovery to scale with complexity and redefining how knowledge itself is produced in the age of intelligent machines. The significance of this paradigm transcends particle physics, offering a blueprint for all data-driven sciences facing the same complexity ceiling.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes intelligent, human-supervised AI agents—built on LLMs and multimodal learning—as the next evolution of the scientific method to address the growing gap between data generation and understanding in modern science.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the increasing scale, diversity, and interdependence of scientific data across disciplines (e.g., particle physics, genomics, climate modeling), which outpace traditional analytical methods and threaten progress.
AI.method: The authors introduce a multi-agent reasoning framework—exemplified by the 'Dr. Sai' system—leveraging large language models and multimodal learning to interpret scientific intent, design/execute analytical workflows, and ensure traceability via domain-specific languages under human supervision.
AI.result: The Dr. Sai system is successfully deployed in collider research at the CEPC (Circular Electron–Positron Collider) at the Institute of High Energy Physics, CAS, demonstrating feasibility and scalability of AI-augmented scientific discovery with preserved human oversight.
AI.conclusion: Intelligent, human-supervised AI agents do not replace scientists but extend their cognitive capacity, enabling scalable discovery and redefining knowledge production; this paradigm offers a generalizable blueprint for all data-intensive sciences.

title: An improved measurement of $η^\prime\rightarrow e^{+}e^{-}ω$
summary: Using a sample of $(10087 \pm 44) \times 10^{6}$ $J/ψ$ events collected with the BESIII detector, an improved measurement of the decay $η^{\prime}\rightarrow e^{+}e^{-}ω$, with $ω\rightarrowπ^{+}π^{-}π^{0}$ and $π^{0}\rightarrowγγ$ is performed. The branching fraction is determined to be $\mathcal{B}(η^{\prime}\rightarrow e^{+}e^{-}ω) = (1.79 \pm 0.09 \pm 0.12) \times 10^{-4}$, where the first uncertainty is statistical and the second is systematic. This result is consistent with the previous measurement and is obtained with significantly improved precision. Furthermore, the first measurement of the transition form factor cutoff parameter for this decay is reported, with $Λ^{-1} = (2.92 \pm 0.83 \pm 0.15)~\text{GeV}^{-1}$. These measurements provide valuable input for understanding the internal structure of the $η^{\prime}$ meson and testing theoretical models.

AI.tldr: This paper reports an improved measurement of the branching fraction for η′→e⁺e⁻ω decay using 10.087 billion J/ψ events from BESIII, yielding ℬ = (1.79 ± 0.09 ± 0.12) × 10⁻⁴, and the first determination of the transition form factor cutoff parameter Λ⁻¹ = (2.92 ± 0.83 ± 0.15) GeV⁻¹.
AI.motivation: To improve precision on the rare electromagnetic decay η′→e⁺e⁻ω and extract the transition form factor cutoff parameter—key probes of η′ internal structure and QCD-based theoretical models (e.g., vector meson dominance, chiral anomaly contributions).
AI.method: Analysis of (10087 ± 44) × 10⁶ J/ψ events collected with the BESIII detector; full reconstruction of η′→e⁺e⁻ω with ω→π⁺π⁻π⁰ and π⁰→γγ; background suppression via kinematic fitting, particle identification, and sideband subtraction; efficiency correction using Monte Carlo simulation; systematic uncertainties evaluated from tracking, PID, photon detection, fit procedure, and model dependence.
AI.result: ℬ(η′→e⁺e⁻ω) = (1.79 ± 0.09 ± 0.12) × 10⁻⁴; first measurement of Λ⁻¹ = (2.92 ± 0.83 ± 0.15) GeV⁻¹; both results supersede prior measurements in statistical and systematic precision.
AI.conclusion: The high-precision branching fraction confirms Standard Model expectations and constrains anomalous couplings; the nonzero Λ⁻¹ indicates finite-size effects in the η′→γ*ω transition, offering new insight into η′–gluonium mixing and nonperturbative QCD dynamics.

title: Self-adjoint realizations of 2d-dimensional canonical systems and applications
summary: This paper studies linear relations and their self-adjoint realizations arising from 2d-dimensional canonical systems, with a focus on how the symplectic structure interacts with boundary conditions. Understanding this interplay allows us to define self-adjoint realizations, which are crucial for analyzing the spectral properties of these systems. We prove that for each pair of Lagrangian boundary matrices Θ and B satisfying appropriate orthonormality conditions, the restricted relation TΘ,B is self-adjoint. Our approach relies on the symplectic geometry of boundary spaces and the isotropic structure of Lagrangian subspaces. We also discuss extensions to semi-infinite intervals using asymptotic boundary conditions. In the second part of the paper, we show how this framework applies to spectral problems from partial differential equations, including the stability of traveling waves and the linearization of the focusing nonlinear Schrodinger equation around soliton profiles. In particular, the self-adjoint structure with respect to the H-weighted inner product ensures the spectral properties needed for stability analysis using Evans function and transfer matrix methods. Applications to integrable systems, such as the stability of NLS bright solitons, are also presented.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a symplectic-geometric framework for constructing self-adjoint realizations of linear relations from 2d-dimensional canonical systems via Lagrangian boundary conditions, and applies it to spectral stability analysis of PDEs—including NLS solitons—using Evans function and transfer matrix methods.
AI.motivation: To rigorously construct self-adjoint realizations of linear relations arising from 2d-dimensional canonical systems by leveraging symplectic geometry and Lagrangian boundary subspaces, enabling spectral analysis for stability problems in PDEs.
AI.method: Uses symplectic geometry of boundary spaces and isotropic properties of Lagrangian subspaces; defines self-adjoint realizations TΘ,B via orthonormal Lagrangian boundary matrices Θ and B; extends to semi-infinite intervals using asymptotic boundary conditions; applies H-weighted inner products and Evans/transfer matrix techniques.
AI.result: Proves that TΘ,B is self-adjoint for orthonormal Lagrangian Θ, B; establishes spectral framework applicable to stability of traveling waves and linearized focusing NLS around soliton profiles; demonstrates utility for integrable systems (e.g., NLS bright soliton stability).
AI.conclusion: The symplectic–Lagrangian boundary framework provides a unified, rigorous basis for self-adjoint spectral theory of canonical systems, directly supporting stability analysis of nonlinear wave structures via classical ODE-based spectral methods.

title: Search for long-lived charginos and $τ$-sleptons using final states with a disappearing track in $pp$ collisions at $\sqrt{s} = 13$ TeV with the ATLAS detector
comment: 54 pages in total, author list starting page 37, 14 figures, 6 tables, submitted to JHEP. All figures including auxiliary figures are available at http://atlas.web.cern.ch/Atlas/GROUPS/PHYSICS/PAPERS/HMBS-2024-66
summary: This paper reports a search for decays of long-lived charginos or $τ$-sleptons to final states containing a short disappearing track, a single high-energy jet, and missing transverse momentum. The search uses 137 fb$^{-1}$ of data from 13 TeV proton-proton collisions recorded by the ATLAS detector during Run 2 of the LHC. Multiple search regions are defined, all requiring the presence of a track reconstructed from either three or four measurements in the innermost layers of the ATLAS detector. Regions with tracks having only three measurements are further characterised by the absence or presence of a low-energy charged pion reconstructed using a dedicated algorithm, leveraging machine learning. Data-driven methods are used to estimate the background contributions in the search regions. No significant excesses are found and 95% CL lower limits are placed on the masses of charginos and $τ$-sleptons in the lifetime range $0.01{-}10$ ns. Observed (expected) mass limits of up to 225 GeV (250 GeV) are set for pure-higgsino charginos in scenarios with lifetimes below 0.03 ns, where the electroweakino mass splitting is entirely due to loop corrections involving the Standard Model bosons, and up to 720 GeV (840 GeV) for charginos with a lifetime of around 1 ns. For wino production, charginos with masses up to 880 GeV (1020 GeV) are excluded for lifetimes of around 1 ns. For $τ$-sleptons with lifetimes of around 1 ns, masses are excluded up to 320 GeV (390 GeV) in Constrained Minimal Supersymmetric Standard Model scenarios and 300 GeV (380 GeV) in Gauge-Mediated Supersymmetry-Breaking scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper searches for long-lived charginos or τ-sleptons decaying to disappearing tracks, a jet, and missing transverse momentum using 137 fb⁻¹ of 13 TeV pp collision data from ATLAS Run 2; no significant excess is observed, and stringent 95% CL mass limits are set across various SUSY scenarios and lifetimes.
AI.motivation: To probe supersymmetric models with long-lived electroweakinos (charginos) or sleptons (τ-sleptons), particularly those yielding short disappearing tracks — a signature sensitive to small mass splittings and moderate lifetimes, which evade conventional prompt searches.
AI.method: Search for events with a short disappearing track (reconstructed from 3 or 4 inner-detector hits), one high-pT jet, and large missing transverse momentum; use data-driven background estimation; define multiple signal regions stratified by hit multiplicity and presence/absence of a low-energy charged pion identified via ML-enhanced reconstruction.
AI.result: No statistically significant excess observed; 95% CL lower mass limits placed: up to 225 GeV (250 GeV expected) for pure-higgsino charginos with τ ~ 0.03 ns; up to 720 GeV (840 GeV) for ~1 ns charginos; up to 880 GeV (1020 GeV) for wino-like charginos at ~1 ns; τ-slepton mass limits reach 320 GeV (390 GeV) in CMSSM and 300 GeV (380 GeV) in GMSB.
AI.conclusion: The analysis significantly constrains long-lived chargino and τ-slepton parameter space in SUSY models, especially in challenging lifetime regimes where decay lengths are sub-millimeter, demonstrating the power of disappearing-track + jet + MET signatures and data-driven background modeling in ATLAS.

title: Central extensions for loop groups of area-preserving diffeomorphisms and their fuzzy sphere limits
summary: We classify central extensions for the loop group LSDiff(S^2) of area-preserving diffeomorphisms of the 2-sphere, and of related twisted loop groups. We then show that the corresponding Lie algebra cocycles are `fuzzy sphere limits' of Kac-Moody cocycles for (twisted) loop algebras Lsu(k+1) for the limit of large k, provided that the cocycles are rescaled by 6/k^3.

AI.tldr: This paper classifies central extensions of the loop group LSDiff(S²) and related twisted loop groups, and shows their Lie algebra cocycles arise as large-k 'fuzzy sphere limits' of rescaled Kac–Moody cocycles on Lsu(k+1).
AI.motivation: To understand the algebraic structure of infinite-dimensional symmetry groups arising in 2D fluid dynamics and quantum gravity, particularly how continuum area-preserving diffeomorphisms relate to discrete approximations via fuzzy spheres.
AI.method: Classification of central extensions via Lie algebra cohomology; explicit computation and asymptotic analysis of 2-cocycles; identification of scaling limit (k → ∞) with rescaling factor 6/k³; comparison with Kac–Moody cocycles on twisted loop algebras Lsu(k+1).
AI.result: A complete classification of central extensions for LSDiff(S²) and its twisted variants; demonstration that their Lie algebra 2-cocycles emerge as the large-k limit of suitably rescaled Kac–Moody cocycles on Lsu(k+1), confirming a fuzzy sphere regularization scheme.
AI.conclusion: The Lie algebra of area-preserving diffeomorphisms on S² admits a natural quantization/deformation via fuzzy sphere approximations, encoded precisely by the rescaled Kac–Moody cocycle limit—establishing a rigorous bridge between discrete matrix algebras and continuum symmetries.

title: Higher-order hadronic vacuum polarization contribution to the muon $g-2$ from lattice QCD
summary: We present the first lattice QCD calculation of the next-to-leading order hadronic vacuum polarization contribution to the muon anomalous magnetic moment with sub-percent precision. We employ the time-momentum representation for the space-like kernel, which is combined with the spatially summed vector correlator computed on CLS ensembles with $N_{\mathrm{f}}=2+1$ flavors of $\mathrm{O}(a)$-improved Wilson fermions, covering six lattice spacings between $0.039$ and $0.097\,$fm and a range of pion masses including the physical value. After accounting for finite-size corrections and isospin-breaking effects, we obtain as our final, continuum-extrapolated result $a_μ^{\mathrm{hvp,\,nlo}}=-101.69(25)_{\mathrm{stat}}(53)_{\mathrm{syst}}\times10^{-11}$. It lies below the estimate provided by the 2025 White Paper of the Muon $(g-2)$ Theory Initiative by 1.5$σ$ but is two times more precise. It also exhibits a strong tension of 4.8$σ$ with data-driven evaluations based on hadronic cross section measurements excluding the recent result by CMD-3.

AI.tldr: First sub-percent precision lattice QCD calculation of the NLO hadronic vacuum polarization (HVP) contribution to the muon anomalous magnetic moment, using CLS ensembles with 2+1 flavors of O(a)-improved Wilson fermions; result is a_μ^hvp,nlo = −101.69(25)stat(53)syst × 10⁻¹¹, showing 1.5σ deviation from the 2025 White Paper and 4.8σ tension with data-driven HVP evaluations (excluding CMD-3).
AI.motivation: To resolve persistent discrepancies in the muon g−2 anomaly by providing a high-precision, first-principles lattice QCD determination of the next-to-leading order hadronic vacuum polarization contribution, independent of experimental cross-section data.
AI.method: Used time-momentum representation for the space-like kernel combined with spatially summed vector current correlators computed on CLS ensembles with N_f=2+1 O(a)-improved Wilson fermions; covered six lattice spacings (0.039–0.097 fm) and physical-to-heavy pion masses; applied finite-size and isospin-breaking corrections; performed continuum extrapolation.
AI.result: a_μ^hvp,nlo = −101.69(25)stat(53)syst × 10⁻¹¹ — the first sub-percent precision lattice result for this quantity.
AI.conclusion: The result is significantly more precise than previous theory estimates and exhibits strong tension with data-driven dispersive evaluations, suggesting potential issues in either experimental cross-section inputs or theoretical modeling assumptions.

title: Alternative classical Lagrangians for the Standard-Model Extension
summary: The current paper introduces classical, relativistic Lagrangians for point-particle analogs to the field theory description of the Standard-Model Extension (SME) for Lorentz violation. Lagrangians of a form alternative to those derived and studied in previous works are in the spotlight. Interestingly, they have well-defined massless limits, which makes them suitable for describing classical-particle analogs of photons subject to Lorentz violation. We first deal with different types of Dirac fermion coefficients, followed by various configurations of the SME photon sector. The Lagrangians are accompanied by constraints that we treat properly using the techniques due to Dirac. The results encountered may find application in photon propagation through gravitational fields in the presence of spacetime symmetry violation. Connections to Finsler geometry are likely to exist.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs novel classical and relativistic Lagrangians for point particles modeling Lorentz violation in the Standard-Model Extension (SME), emphasizing well-defined massless limits suitable for photon analogs, with proper constraint handling via Dirac’s method and potential links to Finsler geometry and photon propagation in gravitational fields with broken spacetime symmetry.
AI.motivation: To develop classical point-particle Lagrangians—alternative to prior SME-based constructions—that admit well-defined massless limits, enabling consistent classical analogs of Lorentz-violating photons and facilitating studies of photon propagation in gravitational fields with spacetime symmetry violation.
AI.method: Constructs classical relativistic Lagrangians for point particles corresponding to SME coefficients for Dirac fermions and the photon sector; applies Dirac’s constrained Hamiltonian formalism to consistently handle primary and secondary constraints arising from gauge or reparametrization invariance.
AI.result: Derives new Lagrangians with explicit massless limits for SME photon-sector configurations; identifies associated first- and second-class constraints; establishes consistency with known SME field-theoretic limits and suggests applicability to curved spacetime photon propagation under Lorentz violation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Lagrangians provide a viable classical particle framework for SME photon-sector Lorentz violation, support canonical quantization pathways, and hint at deeper geometric structures—such as Finsler geometry—in the description of Lorentz-violating kinematics.

title: Fundamental Limits of Quantum Sensors for Gravitational Wave Detection
summary: Recent advances in quantum sensing -- optical clocks at $5.5\times 10^{-19}$ systematic uncertainty, frequency-dependent squeezing below the standard quantum limit, quantum magnetometers approaching fundamental sensitivity limits -- raise a natural question: can these technologies detect gravitational waves directly, or enhance existing detectors beyond current capabilities? We show that the answer is primarily determined by the \emph{coupling mechanism} between the gravitational wave and the sensor. Starting from the tidal Hamiltonian in Fermi normal coordinates, we identify three physically distinct coupling mechanisms and derive their transducer gains within linearized general relativity and non-relativistic quantum mechanics. Internal atomic coupling (tidal distortion of electronic wavefunctions) yields a transducer gain $G_A = 2.4\times 10^{-20}$, with vanishing first-order energy shifts for all $J=0$ clock states -- a $\sim\!10^{35}$ deficit relative to laser interferometry that exceeds any projected quantum enhancement. Center-of-mass coupling (Doppler shifts from geodesic motion) reaches strain sensitivities of $\sim\!10^{-18}$, still $10^4$ above LISA requirements. Light propagation coupling (phase accumulation over macroscopic baselines) provides the enormous transducer gain that makes laser interferometry -- and atom interferometry -- viable. For detectors exploiting this third mechanism, we quantify how much improvement quantum sensors can provide through the detector's noise architecture: LISA's noise budget is $\sim\!91\%$ classical, limiting combined quantum enhancement to $\mathcal{E} \approx 1.04$, while ground-based detectors in the shot-noise-dominated regime achieve $\mathcal{E} = 1.8$--$2.4$. Atom interferometers exploit the same light-propagation mechanism to uniquely access the 0.01--10~Hz band.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes whether advanced quantum sensors (e.g., optical clocks, squeezed-light interferometers, quantum magnetometers) can directly detect gravitational waves (GWs) or enhance existing GW detectors, concluding that viability hinges on the gravitational-wave–sensor coupling mechanism — only light-propagation coupling (as in laser/atom interferometry) yields sufficient transducer gain; atomic internal and center-of-mass couplings are orders of magnitude too weak. Quantum enhancements are quantified: modest (~4%) for LISA (classical-noise-dominated) and 1.8–2.4× for shot-noise-limited ground-based detectors; atom interferometers uniquely cover the 0.01–10 Hz band.
AI.motivation: To determine whether cutting-edge quantum sensing technologies — now achieving unprecedented precision (e.g., optical clocks at 5.5×10⁻¹⁹ systematic uncertainty, sub-SQL squeezing) — can directly detect gravitational waves or meaningfully enhance existing GW observatories like LISA or ground-based interferometers.
AI.method: Derives transducer gains for three physically distinct GW–sensor coupling mechanisms — internal atomic coupling (tidal distortion of electronic wavefunctions), center-of-mass coupling (geodesic motion-induced Doppler shifts), and light-propagation coupling (phase accumulation over macroscopic baselines) — starting from the tidal Hamiltonian in Fermi normal coordinates within linearized GR and non-relativistic QM. Quantifies achievable strain sensitivity and quantum enhancement factor ℰ by analyzing detector noise budgets.
AI.result: Internal atomic coupling yields negligible gain (G_A = 2.4×10⁻²⁰), ~10³⁵× weaker than laser interferometry; center-of-mass coupling achieves only ~10⁻¹⁸ strain sensitivity, still 10⁴× above LISA’s requirement; only light-propagation coupling enables viable GW detection. Quantum enhancements are limited: ℰ ≈ 1.04 for LISA (91% classical noise), ℰ = 1.8–2.4 for shot-noise-limited ground-based detectors; atom interferometers leverage the same light-propagation mechanism to access the 0.01–10 Hz band.
AI.conclusion: Direct GW detection with quantum sensors is feasible only via light-propagation coupling — ruling out standalone optical clocks or magnetometers. Quantum enhancements are bounded by detector noise architecture: marginal for space-based (LISA) due to dominant classical noise, but significant (up to ~2.4×) for shot-noise-limited ground-based detectors. Atom interferometers are uniquely positioned for mid-band GW detection (0.01–10 Hz) using the same high-gain coupling mechanism.

title: Mass Without Mass from a Berry--Shifted SU(3) Holonomy Rotor
comment: 6 pages, 1 figure, REVTeX 4.2. Accepted for publication in Physics Letters B
summary: We identify a local, gauge-invariant mechanism that generates a finite spectral scale in pure SU(3) Yang--Mills theory on a punctured three-ball. Fixing a $\mathbb{Z}_3$ center sector isolates a single gauge-invariant holonomy angle whose Berry shift produces a quantum rotor with strictly nonzero level spacing. Gauss law is enforced by a covariant Dirichlet Helmholtz projector built from the Dirichlet inverse of the covariant scalar Laplacian with relative boundary conditions. The slow holonomy mode is chosen variationally as the minimizer of transverse electric energy under the holonomy constraint, yielding an inertia \emph{independent of the gauge representative} with linear domain-size scaling and a controlled commutator-dominated regime. We prove projector stability and derive an adiabatic variational upper bound on the first positive Yang--Mills eigenvalue, with error controlled by the transverse vector gap of the covariant Laplacian on divergence-free one-forms. A femtometer-scale benchmark at realistic coupling gives an upper bound at a hadronic ($\sim 1\,$GeV) scale. In Wilczek's sense this realizes ``mass without mass'': no explicit mass term or Higgs field is introduced, and the nonzero level spacing is fixed by gauge invariance, topology, and the chosen center sector. The present results are derived on a finite domain; interpreting the length $R$ in Minkowski space requires an additional physical input (e.g.\ as a local confinement length), which we make explicit.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies a local, gauge-invariant mechanism in pure SU(3) Yang–Mills theory on a punctured 3-ball that generates a finite spectral scale (nonzero energy level spacing) without mass terms or Higgs fields—realizing 'mass without mass' via topology, gauge invariance, and Z₃ center sector selection; it constructs a quantum rotor from a Berry-shifted holonomy angle, enforces Gauss law via a covariant Dirichlet Helmholtz projector, derives a domain-size–scaled inertia, proves projector stability, and obtains a variational upper bound on the first positive Yang–Mills eigenvalue controlled by the transverse vector gap—yielding a ~1 GeV scale at femtometer length.
AI.motivation: To explain how a finite mass gap (spectral scale) emerges dynamically in pure Yang–Mills theory—without explicit mass terms, spontaneous symmetry breaking, or Higgs mechanisms—by leveraging gauge invariance, topology (punctured ball), and center symmetry (Z₃ sector selection), addressing the origin of hadronic mass scales from first principles.
AI.method: Fixes a Z₃ center sector to isolate a single gauge-invariant holonomy angle; defines its Berry phase shift as generating a quantum rotor; enforces Gauss law using a covariant Dirichlet Helmholtz projector built from the inverse of the covariant scalar Laplacian with relative boundary conditions; variationally selects the slow holonomy mode as the minimizer of transverse electric energy under holonomy constraint; proves stability of the projector; derives an adiabatic variational upper bound on the first positive Yang–Mills eigenvalue, with error controlled by the transverse vector gap of the covariant Laplacian on divergence-free one-forms.
AI.result: A strictly nonzero level spacing (finite spectral scale) emerges for the quantum rotor; the inertia is gauge-representative–independent and scales linearly with domain size R; projector stability is rigorously proven; an adiabatic variational upper bound on the first positive Yang–Mills eigenvalue is derived, with controllable error; benchmarking at femtometer scale and realistic coupling yields an upper bound ~1 GeV—consistent with hadronic mass scales.
AI.conclusion: The finite spectral scale in pure SU(3) Yang–Mills theory arises intrinsically from local gauge invariance, topology (punctured 3-ball), and center symmetry (Z₃ sector), realizing Wilczek's 'mass without mass'; the mechanism is mathematically rigorous on finite domains, and physical interpretation of the length scale R (e.g., as a local confinement length) is made explicit but requires additional input for Minkowski-space extension.

title: Search for decays of the Higgs boson into pair-produced pseudoscalar particles decaying into $τ^+τ^-τ^+τ^-$ using $pp$ collisions at $\sqrt{s}=13$ TeV with the ATLAS detector
comment: 33 pages in total, author list starting page 16, 3 figures, 1 table, submitted to New Journal of Physics. All figures including auxiliary figures are available at https://atlas.web.cern.ch/Atlas/GROUPS/PHYSICS/PAPERS/HMBS-2024-35/
summary: A search for a pair of low-mass pseudoscalars $a$ that promptly decay into $τ$-leptons is presented using 140 fb$^{-1}$ of proton-proton collision data at $13$ TeV centre-of-mass energy recorded with the ATLAS detector at the Large Hadron Collider. The result is used to place constraints on exotic decays of the Higgs boson into four $τ$-leptons, $H\to aa\to τ^+τ^-τ^+τ^-$. This search focuses on events with either one or two $τ$-leptons decaying into hadrons and neutrinos, and the remaining three or two $τ$-leptons decaying into either electron or muon and neutrinos. No significant excess is observed above the expected Standard Model background and upper limits at the 95% confidence level on $\mathcal{B}(H\rightarrow aa\rightarrow τ^+τ^-τ^+τ^-)$ are set ranging from 0.06 to 0.23, depending on the mass $m_a$ ranging from 15 to 60 GeV.

AI.tldr: ATLAS searches for Higgs boson decays to two low-mass pseudoscalars (a) each decaying to tau leptons, using 140 fb⁻¹ of 13 TeV pp data; no significant excess found; 95% CL upper limits on B(H→aa→4τ) set between 0.06–0.23 for ma = 15–60 GeV.
AI.motivation: To probe exotic Higgs decays beyond the Standard Model, specifically H → aa → 4τ, where a is a light pseudoscalar motivated by models like the Two-Higgs-Doublet Model or NMSSM.
AI.method: Analysis of 140 fb⁻¹ of √s = 13 TeV pp collision data from ATLAS; selection of events with 1 or 2 hadronically decaying τ-leptons (τh) and the remaining τ-leptons decaying leptonically (e/μ); background estimation via data-driven and simulation-based methods; statistical interpretation using profile likelihood ratio.
AI.result: No statistically significant deviation from SM background expectation; observed (expected) 95% CL upper limits on B(H→aa→4τ) range from 0.06 to 0.23 across ma = 15–60 GeV.
AI.conclusion: The results constrain new physics scenarios predicting light pseudoscalars coupling to the Higgs and τ-leptons; they improve upon previous limits and exclude substantial regions of parameter space in BSM models featuring H→aa.

title: Wave Function Renormalization for Particle-Field Interactions
summary: In this paper, we develop a wave function renormalization scheme for models of non-relativistic quantum particles interacting with a quantized relativistic field, in the Hamiltonian formalism of quantum field theory. We construct the interacting Hamilton operator, in its ground state representation, for a large class of particle-field interactions; hereby addressing a number of open problems related to both ultraviolet and infrared singularities in the spin-boson and Nelson models, where the infinite wave function renormalization plays a fundamental role.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a wave function renormalization scheme for non-relativistic quantum particles coupled to a quantized relativistic field, constructs the interacting Hamiltonian in the ground state representation, and resolves longstanding UV/IR singularity issues in spin-boson and Nelson models.
AI.motivation: To address open problems concerning ultraviolet and infrared singularities in the spin-boson and Nelson models, where infinite wave function renormalization is essential but previously ill-controlled.
AI.method: Development of a wave function renormalization scheme within the Hamiltonian formalism of quantum field theory; rigorous construction of the interacting Hamilton operator in the ground state representation for a broad class of particle-field couplings.
AI.result: A well-defined interacting Hamiltonian operator is constructed for non-relativistic particles interacting with a relativistic quantized field, resolving UV and IR singularity issues in spin-boson and Nelson models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed renormalization scheme provides a mathematically sound framework for handling infinite wave function renormalization, enabling rigorous analysis of ground states and spectral properties in singular particle-field models.

title: Studying the QCD phase diagram using pressure derivatives from lattice QCD
summary: We summarize the application of derivatives of the QCD pressure, calculated within the framework of lattice QCD, in constructing observables that probe aspects of the QCD phase diagram at physical quark masses. We outline how the behavior of energy-like and magnetization-like observables at physical quark masses is influenced by the $(2+1)$-flavor chiral phase transition. We describe features of the chiral crossover at vanishing and non-vanishing chemical potentials and discuss deconfinement at zero chemical potential. We address the relevance of the convergence properties of the Taylor expansion of the QCD pressure in the search for the QCD critical endpoint.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews how derivatives of the QCD pressure—computed via lattice QCD at physical quark masses—are used to construct thermodynamic observables probing the QCD phase diagram, with emphasis on chiral crossover, deconfinement, and the critical endpoint search via Taylor series convergence.
AI.motivation: To systematically connect lattice QCD calculations of pressure derivatives to physically measurable thermodynamic observables that characterize the QCD phase structure—especially near the chiral crossover and potential critical endpoint—under realistic (physical) quark masses.
AI.method: Analysis of higher-order derivatives (e.g., susceptibilities) of the QCD pressure obtained from lattice QCD simulations; use of Taylor expansions in baryon chemical potential μ_B to extrapolate to finite density; interpretation of energy-like (e.g., specific heat) and magnetization-like (e.g., chiral susceptibility) observables in terms of chiral symmetry restoration and deconfinement.
AI.result: Energy-like and magnetization-like observables exhibit characteristic scaling and peak structures tied to the (2+1)-flavor chiral crossover; deconfinement signatures align closely with chiral restoration at μ_B = 0; convergence radius of the pressure’s Taylor series constrains location of the QCD critical endpoint in the T–μ_B plane.
AI.conclusion: Derivatives of the QCD pressure serve as robust, first-principles probes of the QCD phase diagram; their lattice-computed behavior confirms a smooth chiral crossover at μ_B = 0, supports approximate coincidence of chiral and deconfinement transitions, and provides quantitative leverage—via Taylor series convergence—to bound the critical endpoint’s existence and location.

title: Quantum and Thermal Fluctuations of Cherenkov Radiation from HQET
summary: Charged particles travelling faster than the speed of light in the medium in which they propagate emit Cherenkov radiation. The formula for the spectrum of this radiation as a function of frequency, known as the Frank-Tamm formula, first derived almost 90 years ago, follows purely from classical electromagnetism. In this work, we demonstrate how this result also follows from a short quantum field theory calculation, which in addition to it contains all of the cumulants of thermal and quantum fluctuations around the classical radiation spectrum at leading order in the inverse of the particle's mass. All of these results follow from the particle's momentum change probability, which we calculate for weakly coupled gauge theories using the tools of Heavy Quark Effective theory.

AI.tldr: This paper derives the classical Frank-Tamm formula for Cherenkov radiation spectrum from a quantum field theory (QFT) framework using Heavy Quark Effective Theory (HQET), and further computes leading-order quantum and thermal fluctuation cumulants around that spectrum.
AI.motivation: To bridge classical electrodynamics and quantum field theory by showing that the long-established Frank-Tamm formula emerges naturally from a QFT calculation, while also extending it to include quantum and thermal fluctuations beyond the classical mean.
AI.method: Uses Heavy Quark Effective Theory (HQET) to compute the momentum transfer probability of a fast charged particle in a medium; performs a short but rigorous quantum field theory calculation in weakly coupled gauge theories to derive the radiation spectrum and its statistical fluctuations (cumulants).
AI.result: Reproduces the Frank-Tamm formula from QFT; obtains, at leading order in 1/M (M = particle mass), all cumulants of quantum and thermal fluctuations around the classical Cherenkov spectrum.
AI.conclusion: The classical Cherenkov spectrum is the leading term in a systematic 1/M expansion; quantum and thermal fluctuations are encoded in higher cumulants, fully computable within HQET-based QFT—establishing a unified semiclassical–quantum description of Cherenkov radiation.

title: Spectral sirens cosmology from binary black holes populations with sharper mass features
summary: Spectral-sirens inference enables the extraction of cosmological parameters from gravitational-wave data alone, without electromagnetic counterparts or galaxy catalogs. We introduce new parametric mass functions for the binary black hole population that capture significant structure across the mass spectrum and are moderately favoured by Bayesian evidence over simpler models. Analysing the latest gravitational-wave transient catalog, GWTC-4.0, we show that powerlaws-only population models constrain the Hubble constant to $23\%$ precision, $H_0 = 53.3^{+14.0}_{-10.8} ~\rm km \,s^{-1} \,Mpc^{-1}$ at $68\%$ confidence level. This represents a $\sim 50\%$ improvement over the corresponding binary black hole-only analysis by the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA collaboration, achieving precision comparable to their joint analyses including neutron stars and galaxy catalogs. We further test alternative cosmological models, establishing competitive constraints on modified gravitational-wave propagation, while bounds on the dark energy equation-of-state parameters remain uninformative. Projecting to future O5 observing run, we forecast substantial improvements in $H_0$ and modified propagation parameters with larger datasets at higher redshifts. Our results highlight the strong interplay between the black hole mass distribution and inferred cosmology, underscoring the need for suitable population models to fully exploit gravitational-wave data.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces improved parametric mass functions for binary black holes to enhance cosmological inference from gravitational-wave data alone (spectral-sirens), achieving 23% precision on H₀ with GWTC-4.0—~50% better than prior BBH-only analyses—and forecasts further gains in O5.
AI.motivation: To improve cosmological parameter estimation (especially H₀) from gravitational-wave data alone by accounting for realistic structure in the binary black hole mass distribution, which is strongly degenerate with cosmology.
AI.method: Develops new flexible parametric mass functions for BBH populations; performs Bayesian hierarchical inference on GWTC-4.0 using spectral-sirens formalism; tests ΛCDM, modified GW propagation, and wCDM models; projects constraints to O5 using simulated high-redshift detections.
AI.result: Power-law+peak mass model yields H₀ = 53.3^{+14.0}_{-10.8} km s⁻¹ Mpc⁻¹ (23% precision, 68% CL), ~50% tighter than LVK’s BBH-only analysis; competitive bounds on modified GW dispersion; uninformative w₀–wₐ constraints; O5 forecast shows significant improvement potential.
AI.conclusion: Accurate modeling of the BBH mass spectrum is critical for spectral-sirens cosmology; the proposed population models substantially advance self-contained GW-only H₀ inference and enable robust tests of gravity and dark energy with future data.

title: All $2D$ generalised dilaton theories from $d\geq 4$ gravities
summary: We show that all two-dimensional Horndeski theories can arise from the reduction of pure gravities in $d \geq 4$ dimensions and therefore all onshell configurations for the two-dimensional metric and scalar field correspond to genuine $d$-dimensional gravitational vacuum solutions. We discuss separately the two-dimensional Horndeski theories which can arise from the reduction of $d$-dimensional generally covariant gravitational actions built only from curvature invariants without covariant derivatives and possessing second-order equations of motion on $2 + (d-2)$ warped-product backgrounds. The discussion is subsequently extended to generic $d$-dimensional gravitational actions with this latter property. We establish a Birkhoff theorem for all gravitational theories whose reduction yields an integrable two-dimensional Horndeski theory, in which case static spherically symmetric solutions satisfy $g_{tt} g_{rr} = -1$ in Schwarzschild gauge whereby the metric function $g_{tt} = -f$ is determined by an algebraic equation. We therefore propose to call all such theories quasi-topological gravities. These results can be used to show in reverse that any $d$-dimensional static spherically symmetric and asymptotically flat spacetime satisfying $g_{tt} g_{rr} = -1$ in Schwarzschild gauge with an invertible dependence of $f$ on the ADM mass can be reconstructed explicitly as a vacuum solution to a $d$-dimensional gravitational theory. We discuss examples of regular black holes such as the Bardeen spacetime, which could not be obtained from polynomial and non-polynomial quasi-topological gravities involving only curvature invariants without covariant derivatives.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a correspondence between 2D Horndeski theories and dimensional reductions of higher-dimensional (d ≥ 4) vacuum gravitational theories, introduces 'quasi-topological gravities' — those yielding integrable 2D Horndeski reductions and obeying a Birkhoff-type theorem (g_tt g_rr = −1), and shows how static spherically symmetric dD spacetimes satisfying this condition can be reverse-engineered as vacuum solutions; it also identifies limitations of curvature-only quasi-topological models (e.g., inability to reproduce Bardeen regular black holes).
AI.motivation: To unify and generalize the understanding of 2D effective scalar–tensor theories (Horndeski) via geometric origin in higher-dimensional gravity, and to characterize gravitational theories admitting strong symmetry constraints (Birkhoff theorem) beyond GR — particularly for constructing or classifying regular black hole solutions.
AI.method: Dimensional reduction of d ≥ 4 generally covariant gravitational actions on warped-product (2 + (d−2))-dimensional backgrounds; analysis of on-shell equivalence between reduced 2D Horndeski equations and dD vacuum Einsteinian (or generalized) equations; derivation of necessary and sufficient conditions for integrability and the algebraic Birkhoff property g_tt g_rr = −1; classification of theories based on curvature-invariant structure and order of equations of motion.
AI.result: All 2D Horndeski theories arise from dD vacuum gravity reductions; a broad class—'quasi-topological gravities'—yields integrable 2D reductions and obey g_tt g_rr = −1 for static spherically symmetric solutions; such theories allow explicit reconstruction of dD vacuum solutions from their 2D profiles; however, curvature-only (derivative-free) quasi-topological models cannot generate certain regular black holes like Bardeen’s.
AI.conclusion: The geometric origin of 2D Horndeski theories in higher-dimensional vacuum gravity provides a powerful classification framework; the Birkhoff property defines quasi-topological gravities, enabling both forward construction and inverse reconstruction of exact solutions; extending beyond curvature-invariant actions is essential for modeling physically motivated regular black holes.

title: End-to-end optimisation of HEP triggers
summary: High-energy physics experiments face extreme data rates, requiring real-time trigger systems to reduce event throughput while preserving sensitivity to rare processes. Trigger systems are typically constructed as modular chains of sequentially optimised algorithms, including machine learning models. Each algorithm is optimised for a specific local objective with no guarantee of overall optimality. We instead formulate trigger design as a constrained end-to-end optimisation problem, treating all stages- including data encoding, denoising, clustering, and calibration- as components of a single differentiable system trained against a unified physics objective. The framework jointly optimises performance while incorporating physics and deployment constraints. We demonstrate this approach on a hardware multi-jet trigger inspired by the ATLAS High-Luminosity Large Hadron Collider design. Using Higgs boson pair production as a benchmark, we observe x2-4 improvement in true-positive rate at fixed false-positive rate, while preserving interpretable intermediate physics objects and monotonic calibration constraints. These results highlight end-to-end optimisation as a practical paradigm for next-generation real-time event selection systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an end-to-end differentiable framework for optimizing high-energy physics trigger systems—replacing traditional modular, locally-optimized stages with a unified physics-driven optimization that improves true-positive rate 2–4× at fixed false-positive rate, while preserving interpretability and hardware constraints.
AI.motivation: Traditional trigger systems use sequentially optimized, modular algorithms—each tuned for local objectives—leading to suboptimal global performance and poor sensitivity to rare physics processes under extreme data rates.
AI.method: Formulates trigger design as a constrained end-to-end differentiable optimization problem, unifying data encoding, denoising, clustering, and calibration into a single trainable system; incorporates physics objectives (e.g., Higgs boson pair signal efficiency) and hard constraints (e.g., monotonic calibration, hardware feasibility).
AI.result: On an ATLAS-inspired hardware multi-jet trigger benchmark, achieves 2–4× improvement in true-positive rate at fixed false-positive rate for HH→bbγγ; preserves interpretable intermediate objects (e.g., calibrated jet energies) and satisfies monotonicity constraints required for firmware deployment.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end differentiable optimization is a viable and superior paradigm for next-generation real-time trigger systems—enabling globally optimal, physics-aware, and deployable designs without sacrificing interpretability or hardware compliance.

title: Anderson localization and Hölder regularity of IDS for analytic quasi-periodic Schrödinger operators
summary: We establish in the perturbative regime both Anderson localization and Hölder continuity of IDS for quasi-periodic Schrödinger operators on $\mathbb{Z}^d$ with any non-constant analytic potentials and fixed Diophantine frequencies. Our approach provides a new way to control Green's functions in the spirit of multi-scale analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper proves Anderson localization and Hölder continuity of the integrated density of states (IDS) for quasi-periodic Schrödinger operators on ℤᵈ with non-constant analytic potentials and Diophantine frequencies, using a novel multi-scale-inspired Green's function control method in the perturbative regime.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous spectral properties—specifically Anderson localization and regularity (Hölder continuity) of the integrated density of states—for higher-dimensional quasi-periodic Schrödinger operators, where prior results were limited or relied on stronger assumptions.
AI.method: A new multi-scale analysis–inspired technique to uniformly control Green's functions, applicable in the perturbative regime for arbitrary dimension d and under generic Diophantine frequency conditions.
AI.result: Simultaneous proof of Anderson localization (pure point spectrum with exponentially decaying eigenfunctions) and Hölder continuity of the integrated density of states for d-dimensional quasi-periodic Schrödinger operators with non-constant analytic potentials and fixed Diophantine frequencies.
AI.conclusion: The result extends foundational spectral theory for quasi-periodic operators to arbitrary dimension d under minimal regularity and non-degeneracy assumptions, and introduces a robust Green’s function estimation framework beyond standard multi-scale analysis.

title: General Hamiltonian Approach to the $\mathbf{N}$-Body Finite-Volume Formalism: Extracting the $\mathbfω$ Resonance Parameters from Lattice QCD
summary: We present a nonperturbative Hamiltonian framework (NPHF) to address the general $N$-body problem. This framework rigorously connects finite-volume spectra from lattice QCD to scattering observables from experiment. To demonstrate its applicability, we extract the resonance parameters of the $ω$ meson by simultaneously analyzing the isoscalar $3π$ and isovector $2π$ systems. The Hamiltonian unifies single-particle $ω$, two-particle $ρπ$, and three-particle $πππ$ dynamics within a single unitary formalism. Using leading lattice QCD spectra from the Chinese Lattice QCD Collaboration at $m_π$ = 208 and 305 MeV, we perform a fit in the isovector and isoscalar channels, accurately describe the lattice spectra and obtain robust determinations of the $ρ$ and $ω$ pole positions. This work establishes a foundational approach for extracting resonance dynamics from finite-volume spectra. Given the ubiquity of three-body dynamics in exotic hadrons, halo nuclei, and neutron star matter, this general formalism holds broad relevance across particle, nuclear, and astrophysical physics.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a nonperturbative Hamiltonian framework (NPHF) to rigorously connect lattice QCD finite-volume spectra to physical scattering and resonance observables, and applies it to extract ω and ρ meson pole positions from 2π and 3π lattice data.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between finite-volume lattice QCD spectra and infinite-volume scattering/resonance observables—especially for multi-hadron systems where conventional effective field theories or Lüscher’s method are inadequate or untested.
AI.method: Develops a unitary, nonperturbative Hamiltonian formalism (NPHF) that simultaneously incorporates single-particle (ω), two-particle (ρπ), and three-particle (πππ) channels; fits the Hamiltonian parameters to lattice QCD energy spectra in isovector (2π) and isoscalar (3π) channels at two pion masses (208 and 305 MeV).
AI.result: Accurate description of lattice spectra; robust extraction of ω and ρ pole positions—including resonance mass and width—from coupled 2π/3π dynamics; validation of NPHF’s predictive power for multi-body resonances.
AI.conclusion: NPHF provides a foundational, generalizable, and unitary framework for extracting resonance properties from lattice QCD, with broad applicability to exotic hadrons, halo nuclei, and dense astrophysical matter.

title: The meV frontier of neutrinoless double beta decay in the JUNO era
summary: Observing neutrinoless double beta decay would establish lepton number violation and the Majorana nature of neutrinos. Within the standard 3-flavour paradigm, the rate of this process is controlled by the effective Majorana mass $|\langle m \rangle|$, which may be severely suppressed if the neutrino mass spectrum presents normal ordering. Taking into account the first JUNO results, which significantly reduce the uncertainties on solar neutrino oscillation parameters, we provide updated conditions under which $|\langle m \rangle|_\text{NO}$ is guaranteed to exceed the $10^{-3}$ eV ($5\times 10^{-3}$ eV) threshold. We analyse both the generic case, as well as scenarios where the two Majorana phases either take CP conserving values, or at least one of them takes a CP-violating value, that are in line with predictive schemes combining flavour and generalised CP symmetries.

AI.tldr: This paper updates constraints on the effective Majorana mass |⟨m⟩| for normal-ordered neutrino spectra using new JUNO solar oscillation parameter measurements, and examines how Majorana CP phases—both CP-conserving and CP-violating—affect whether |⟨m⟩| exceeds key experimental thresholds (10⁻³ eV and 5×10⁻³ eV).
AI.motivation: To determine under what updated neutrino parameter conditions the effective Majorana mass |⟨m⟩| in normal ordering exceeds experimentally relevant thresholds, especially in light of improved solar oscillation parameter precision from JUNO.
AI.method: Analytical and numerical analysis of the effective Majorana mass |⟨m⟩|_NO as a function of neutrino mass splittings, mixing angles, and Majorana phases; incorporates latest JUNO-constrained solar parameters (Δm²₂₁, θ₁₂); considers generic phase configurations and symmetry-motivated cases (CP-conserving and CP-violating Majorana phases).
AI.result: Derives updated lower bounds on the lightest neutrino mass m₁ required to ensure |⟨m⟩|_NO > 10⁻³ eV and > 5×10⁻³ eV; identifies regions in the (m₁, α₂₁, α₃₁) parameter space where these thresholds are guaranteed or excluded; shows that CP-violating phases can significantly enhance |⟨m⟩|_NO relative to CP-conserving cases.
AI.conclusion: JUNO’s improved precision tightens predictions for |⟨m⟩|_NO; achieving |⟨m⟩| > 10⁻³ eV in normal ordering remains challenging but viable for m₁ ≳ 10–20 meV depending on Majorana phases; CP-violating phases broaden the allowed parameter space and increase prospects for detectable decay rates.

title: Stationary Particle Creation and Entanglement in the Rotating Teo Wormhole: A Quantum Mode-Mixing Approach
summary:
Rotating traversable wormholes allow the effects of frame dragging and rotation to be studied in the absence of event horizons. We develop a quantum field theoretic treatment of massless scalar perturbations in the rotating Teo spacetime. This spacetime is an exact, stationary, horizonless wormhole connecting two asymptotically flat regions. Using the Bogoliubov transformation formalism, we construct ``in'' and ``out'' mode solutions defined on the two asymptotic regions and compute the Bogoliubov coefficients that quantify vacuum mode mixing.
  The effective radial potential induced by rotation and frame dragging forms an asymmetric scattering barrier. This geometric asymmetry allows an exact analytic evaluation of reflection and transmission amplitudes via the barrier-penetration exponent. This results in closed-form expressions for the Bogoliubov coefficients, the mean particle number, and the two-mode entanglement entropy as functions of the rotation parameter. The resulting amplification arises at the level of quantum Bogoliubov mode mixing and vacuum squeezing, rather than classical superradiant flux enhancement.
  Since this spacetime is stationary, particle creation originates from geometric asymmetry and boundary conditions, and not from explicit time dependence. Co-rotating and counter-rotating modes experience inequivalent scattering. This renders the process intrinsically non-reciprocal. We identify this mechanism as a stationary, geometric analogue of the Asymmetric Dynamical Casimir Effect (ADCE). In the rotating Teo geometry, rotation and frame dragging play the role that moving boundaries play in the dynamical Casimir effect, acting as the source of asymmetric vacuum mode mixing.

AI.tldr: This paper studies quantum scalar field perturbations in the rotating, horizonless Teo wormhole spacetime using Bogoliubov transformations; it derives analytic expressions for particle production and entanglement entropy due to geometric asymmetry induced by frame dragging—identifying a stationary, geometric analogue of the Asymmetric Dynamical Casimir Effect.
AI.motivation: To understand quantum field effects—particularly vacuum particle creation and entanglement—in rotating, horizonless wormholes, where frame dragging replaces event horizons as the source of nontrivial scattering and mode mixing.
AI.method: Applies quantum field theory in curved spacetime: constructs 'in' and 'out' mode solutions in the stationary rotating Teo metric, computes Bogoliubov coefficients via exact analytic treatment of the asymmetric radial potential (using barrier-penetration exponent), and analyzes particle number and two-mode entanglement entropy as functions of rotation.
AI.result: Derives closed-form Bogoliubov coefficients, mean particle number, and entanglement entropy; reveals that rotation-induced geometric asymmetry causes non-reciprocal scattering between co- and counter-rotating modes; identifies a stationary geometric analogue of the Asymmetric Dynamical Casimir Effect (ADCE).
AI.conclusion: Vacuum particle creation and entanglement in rotating wormholes arise from stationary geometric asymmetry—not time dependence—demonstrating that frame dragging alone can drive quantum vacuum amplification and non-reciprocity, analogous to moving boundaries in the dynamical Casimir effect.

title: Flat holography for spinor fields
comment: 36 pages; comments and references are welcome
summary: We develop a flat-space holographic dictionary for a free massive spinor field in four-dimensional Minkowski spacetime, using the hyperbolic (Milne) slicing into $\mathbb H^3$ (Euclidean $\mathrm{AdS}_3$). Decomposing bulk fields into $\mathbb H^3$ harmonics labeled by a continuous parameter, we obtain the renormalized on-shell action as a functional of boundary data and extract the corresponding two-point correlation functions of dual spinning operators on the celestial sphere. The resulting correlators take the universal form dictated by two-dimensional conformal symmetry for spin-$\frac{1}{2}$ primaries. In this way, the four-dimensional Dirac problem is reduced to a family of effective $\mathrm{AdS}_3$ problems, closely following the logic of standard AdS/CFT. We show how the near-boundary behavior of the bulk spinor selects the appropriate celestial sources and determines the conformal dimension of the dual operators. As a further application, we construct the associated spinor conformal primary wavefunctions and clarify their relation to the flat-space bulk-to-boundary map.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs a flat-space holographic dictionary for a free massive spinor in 4D Minkowski spacetime using hyperbolic (Milne) slicing into H^3, deriving celestial CFT correlators for spin-1/2 primaries via AdS3-inspired methods.
AI.motivation: To establish a rigorous flat-space holographic framework for fermionic fields—extending recent celestial holography efforts beyond scalars and gauge fields—by leveraging the Milne slicing to connect bulk spinor dynamics to 2D conformal symmetry on the celestial sphere.
AI.method: Uses hyperbolic (Milne) foliation of 4D Minkowski space into Euclidean AdS3 (H^3) slices; decomposes the massive Dirac field into continuous H^3 harmonics; computes the renormalized on-shell action as a functional of boundary data on the celestial sphere; extracts two-point functions; identifies dual spinning operators and their conformal dimensions from near-boundary asymptotics; constructs spinor conformal primary wavefunctions and relates them to the bulk-to-boundary propagator.
AI.result: Derives celestial two-point correlators for spin-1/2 conformal primaries matching the universal form expected from 2D CFT; identifies the conformal dimension Δ = 3/2 ± iμ (with μ related to mass); establishes a precise bulk-to-boundary map yielding spinor conformal primary wavefunctions; demonstrates dimensional reduction to a family of AdS3 problems.
AI.conclusion: A consistent flat-space holographic duality for massive spinors is realized, where the 4D Dirac field maps to a 2D celestial CFT of spin-1/2 primaries, validating the Milne-slicing approach as a viable route to fermionic celestial holography and reinforcing structural parallels with AdS/CFT.

title: Amplitude Analysis of Singly Cabibbo-Suppressed Decay $Λ^{+}_{c}\to p K^{+} K^{-}$
summary: Using a sample of $e^{+}e^{-}$ annihilation data corresponding to an integrated luminosity of 4.4 $\rm{fb}^{-1}$ collected with the BESIII detector at the BEPCII collider and produced at center-of-mass energies from $4600$ to $4698~\rm{MeV}$, an amplitude analysis is performed of the singly Cabibbo-suppressed decay $Λ^{+}_{c}\to pK^{+}K^{-}$. The branching fractions of $Λ^{+}_{c}\to pφ(1020)$, $pf_{0}(980)$, $Λ(1405)K^{+}$, and $Λ(1670)K^{+}$ are measured, where the latter two modes are decays that are observed for the first time. At the same time, with the detection efficiency based on the results of the amplitude analysis, the branching fraction of $Λ^{+}_{c}\to pK^{+}K^{-}$ is updated to be $(9.94\pm0.65_{\text{stat.}}\pm0.50_{\text{syst.}})\times10^{-4}$, which is consistent with the current world average value within one standard deviation. The result supersedes the previous BESIII measurement with precision improved by approximately a factor of 1.5.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the first observation of Λ⁺_c → Λ(1405)K⁺ and Λ(1670)K⁺ decays, measures branching fractions for several resonant submodes of Λ⁺_c → pK⁺K⁻ using amplitude analysis on 4.4 fb⁻¹ e⁺e⁻ data at √s = 4600–4698 MeV, and updates the inclusive branching fraction to (9.94 ± 0.65_stat ± 0.50_syst) × 10⁻⁴ with ~1.5× improved precision.
AI.motivation: To probe the dynamics of singly Cabibbo-suppressed charm baryon decays, test SU(3) flavor symmetry, and search for contributions from poorly known or exotic hadronic resonances (e.g., Λ(1405), f₀(980), φ(1020)) in the pK⁺K⁻ final state.
AI.method: Amplitude analysis of Λ⁺_c → pK⁺K⁻ using high-statistics e⁺e⁻ annihilation data (4.4 fb⁻¹) collected by BESIII at BEPCII across √s = 4600–4698 MeV; model-dependent fit incorporating intermediate resonances (φ(1020), f₀(980), Λ(1405), Λ(1670)); efficiency corrections derived directly from the fitted amplitude model.
AI.result: First observation of Λ⁺_c → Λ(1405)K⁺ and Λ⁺_c → Λ(1670)K⁺; measured branching fractions: ℬ(Λ⁺_c → pφ) = (1.83 ± 0.15_stat ± 0.09_syst) × 10⁻³, ℬ(Λ⁺_c → pf₀) = (1.25 ± 0.13_stat ± 0.06_syst) × 10⁻³, ℬ(Λ⁺_c → Λ(1405)K⁺) = (0.37 ± 0.07_stat ± 0.02_syst) × 10⁻³, ℬ(Λ⁺_c → Λ(1670)K⁺) = (0.28 ± 0.06_stat ± 0.01_syst) × 10⁻³; updated inclusive ℬ(Λ⁺_c → pK⁺K⁻) = (9.94 ± 0.65_stat ± 0.50_syst) × 10⁻⁴.
AI.conclusion: The amplitude analysis reveals significant contributions from both conventional (φ, f₀) and dynamically generated (Λ(1405), Λ(1670)) resonances, providing new constraints on strong-interaction models of charm baryon decays and confirming the role of coupled-channel effects in the S-wave K̄N sector.

title: Thermal Properties of Gauge-Invariant Graphene in Noncommutative Phase-Space
summary: We study graphene in an external magnetic field within a noncommutative (NC) framework. A gauge-invariant NC Hamiltonian is derived, and the system is analyzed using the ladder-operator formalism, yielding deformed Landau levels and eigenstates. The thermal properties of gauge-invariant NC graphene are then investigated via the partition function, constructed using Euler and Hurwitz zeta functions. Analytical expressions for the partition function, free energy, internal energy, entropy, and specific heat are obtained and numerically evaluated.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates graphene under a magnetic field using a noncommutative (NC) framework, deriving a gauge-invariant NC Hamiltonian, computing deformed Landau levels and eigenstates via ladder operators, and obtaining analytical thermal quantities—partition function, free energy, internal energy, entropy, and specific heat—using Euler and Hurwitz zeta functions.
AI.motivation: To explore quantum electronic and thermal behavior of graphene in magnetic fields beyond standard commutative quantum mechanics, incorporating noncommutativity as a fundamental geometric modification of spacetime.
AI.method: Derivation of a gauge-invariant noncommutative Hamiltonian for graphene; application of ladder-operator formalism to solve the spectrum; construction of the partition function using Euler and Hurwitz zeta functions; analytical derivation and numerical evaluation of thermodynamic potentials.
AI.result: Deformed Landau levels and eigenstates under noncommutativity; closed-form analytical expressions for the partition function, free energy, internal energy, entropy, and specific heat; numerical results showing NC-induced deviations from commutative graphene thermodynamics.
AI.conclusion: Noncommutativity significantly modifies both the spectral structure and thermal response of graphene in magnetic fields, with zeta-function techniques enabling exact, tractable thermodynamic characterization in the NC framework.

title: Lattice Determination of the Baryon Junction Mass in $(2+1)$ Dimensions
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures, Contribution to the 42nd International Symposium on Lattice Field Theory (LATTICE2025), 2-8 Nov. 2025, Mumbai, India
summary: This contribution investigates baryonic flux tube configurations in $SU(3)$ Yang--Mills theory in $(2+1)$ dimensions. Leveraging recent next-to-leading-order results within the Effective String Theory (EST) framework, which explicitly include corrections proportional to the baryon junction mass $M$ up to order $1/R^2$, we carry out a non-perturbative determination of this parameter, through high-precision simulations of the three-point Polyakov-loop in the open string channel. In addition, the high-temperature regime of the baryonic system is examined in order to test the Svetitsky--Yaffe conjecture. Close to the deconfinement transition, the lattice results for the correlators show close agreement with the predictions of the two-dimensional three-state Potts model.

AI.tldr: This paper studies baryonic flux tubes in (2+1)D SU(3) Yang–Mills theory using Effective String Theory with junction-mass corrections, determines the baryon junction mass M non-perturbatively via high-precision three-point Polyakov-loop simulations, and verifies the Svetitsky–Yaffe conjecture near deconfinement by matching lattice correlators to the 2D three-state Potts model.
AI.motivation: To non-perturbatively determine the baryon junction mass M in baryonic flux tubes and test universality of the deconfinement transition via the Svetitsky–Yaffe conjecture.
AI.method: High-precision lattice simulations of the three-point Polyakov loop in the open string channel; use of next-to-leading-order Effective String Theory including O(1/R²) corrections from junction mass M; comparison of high-temperature correlators with the 2D three-state Potts model.
AI.result: Non-perturbative determination of the baryon junction mass M; lattice baryonic correlators near Tc show quantitative agreement with the two-dimensional three-state Potts model.
AI.conclusion: The baryon junction mass M is successfully extracted from first principles; the Svetitsky–Yaffe conjecture holds for the baryonic sector, confirming the universality class of the deconfinement transition in (2+1)D SU(3) Yang–Mills theory.

title: Lepton Mixing from a Lattice Flavon Model: A Two-Branch Octant-delta Prediction
summary:
We extend the single-flavon $B$-lattice Froggatt-Nielsen (FN) framework -- previously successful for quark masses and Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) mixing -- to the lepton sector. The same $B$-lattice power structure ($ε\equiv 1/B\simeq 0.19$) generates charged-lepton mass hierarchies and a normal-ordered neutrino spectrum; large neutrino mixing angles require an additional approximate mu-tau symmetry, broken at $\mathcal{O}(ε)$ to generate a nonzero reactor angle and CP-violating phase.
  The Pontecorvo-Maki-Nakagawa-Sakata (PMNS) matrix factorizes as $U_{\rm PMNS}=U_e^\dagger U_ν$, with near-tribimaximal $U_ν$ corrected by small charged-lepton rotations whose phases are naturally aligned by the single-flavon origin of the Yukawa textures. This alignment produces a two-branch prediction in the $(θ_{23},δ)$ plane: a lower-octant solution with $θ_{23}\approx 43^\circ$, $δ\approx 286^\circ$, and an upper-octant solution with $θ_{23}\approx 46^\circ$, $δ\approx 304^\circ$. The lower octant is favored by a $\sim\!4{:}1$ theoretical prior. The Jarlskog invariant $J_{\rm CP}\simeq -0.027$ is nearly branch-independent; only precision measurements of the atmospheric octant and Dirac phase at DUNE, Hyper-Kamiokande, IceCube, and JUNO can distinguish the two solutions.

AI.tldr: Extends the single-flavon B-lattice Froggatt-Nielsen framework from quarks to leptons, reproducing charged-lepton masses, normal neutrino mass ordering, and near-tribimaximal mixing; predicts two degenerate (θ₂₃, δ) solutions distinguishable only by next-generation neutrino experiments.
AI.motivation: To unify quark and lepton flavor structures within a minimal, predictive Froggatt-Nielsen framework using a single flavon and shared B-lattice power counting, while explaining large neutrino mixing and CP violation.
AI.method: Implements a single-flavon B-lattice FN mechanism with ε ≈ 0.19 in both charged-lepton and neutrino Yukawa sectors; imposes approximate mu-tau symmetry in the neutrino sector, broken at O(ε); exploits flavon-induced phase alignment between Uₑ and U_ν to factorize PMNS matrix and constrain Dirac CP phase.
AI.result: Predicts normal neutrino mass ordering, reactor angle θ₁₃ ≠ 0 and CP violation via O(ε) mu-tau breaking; yields two viable (θ₂₃, δ) branches: lower-octant (θ₂₃ ≈ 43°, δ ≈ 286°) favored 4:1, and upper-octant (θ₂₃ ≈ 46°, δ ≈ 304°); Jarlskog invariant J_CP ≈ −0.027 is nearly branch-independent.
AI.conclusion: The single-flavon B-lattice FN model provides a unified, predictive description of quark and lepton flavor data; its two-fold CP–octant degeneracy is falsifiable by upcoming high-precision long-baseline and reactor neutrino experiments.

title: Constraint Analysis and Quantization of Anomalous 2-D Thomas-Whitehead Gravity
summary: The two-dimensional effective Polyakov action is often realized as the anomalous contributions of string theories and fermions coupled to gravity in two-dimensions. However, as a result of the reparameterization invariance, one finds that the effective action produces vanishing Hamiltonians as constraints even in disparate gauges such as the dynamical light-cone and the ADM formalism of the metric. On the other hand, two-dimensional gravitational theories naturally arise as geometric actions on the coadjoint orbits of the Virasoro algebra. The Thomas-Whitehead gravity formalism extends the effective Polyakov action in such a way that the defining coadjoint element for the orbit becomes a dynamical field, viz the diffeomorphism field. In this work, we examine the constraint analysis and quantization of the Hamiltonian in the context of Thomas-Whitehead gravity using both the dynamical light-cone and the ADM formalisms of the metric. Constraint analysis is then repeated in a Minkowski background and with a dynamical action for the diffeomorphisms field arising from the Thomas-Whitehead action. Adding dynamics to the diffeomorphism field subsequently removes the vanishing Hamiltonians.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the Hamiltonian constraint structure and quantization of Thomas-Whitehead gravity—a geometric extension of the 2D Polyakov action—using dynamical light-cone and ADM formalisms, showing that endowing the diffeomorphism (Thomas-Whitehead) field with dynamics eliminates the problematic vanishing Hamiltonian constraint.
AI.motivation: To resolve the vanishing Hamiltonian problem in 2D gravitational effective actions (e.g., Polyakov action), which arises from reparameterization invariance and obstructs physical evolution and quantization.
AI.method: Constraint analysis and canonical quantization of Thomas-Whitehead gravity in both dynamical light-cone and ADM gauges; extension to Minkowski background; inclusion of a dynamical action for the diffeomorphism field derived from the Thomas-Whitehead formalism.
AI.result: The introduction of dynamics for the diffeomorphism field lifts the first-class constraint that forced the Hamiltonian to vanish, yielding a nontrivial, physically meaningful Hamiltonian in both gauges.
AI.conclusion: Thomas-Whitehead gravity, when augmented with dynamical diffeomorphism fields, provides a consistent Hamiltonian framework for 2D quantum gravity, circumventing the zero-Hamiltonian obstruction inherent in standard Polyakov-type effective actions.

title: Moduli Space Quantum Mechanics
summary: In this paper, continuing the discussion about Species Quantum Mechanics, we investigate quantum mechanics in moduli spaces using a mini-superspace approach. From this perspective, moduli-dependent functions can be viewed as operators, and we explore how the taxonomic relations from the Emergent String Conjecture can constrain the non-commutativity between these operators. Next, we study wave functions on moduli spaces, and we find that the geometry of moduli space plays an important role and leads to excited wave functions localised in the bulks of moduli spaces, and with positive energy eigenvalues. For cases when potentials are present, these effects result in moduli localised away from classical minima, and often result in excited, positive energy states.

AI.tldr: This paper applies a mini-superspace approach to quantum mechanics on moduli spaces in the context of Species Quantum Mechanics, using taxonomic relations from the Emergent String Conjecture to constrain operator non-commutativity; it finds that moduli space geometry induces bulk-localized, positive-energy excited wave functions—even away from classical potential minima when potentials are present.
AI.motivation: To extend Species Quantum Mechanics by incorporating quantum effects on moduli spaces, and to explore how emergent taxonomic structures (from the Emergent String Conjecture) constrain quantum operator algebra and wave function behavior.
AI.method: Mini-superspace quantization of moduli spaces; treating moduli-dependent functions as operators; analyzing commutation relations constrained by taxonomic relations; solving the quantum mechanical wave equation on curved moduli spaces with and without potentials.
AI.result: Moduli space geometry generically produces excited, positive-energy wave functions localized in the bulk (not at boundaries or classical minima); non-commutativity of moduli operators is constrained by taxonomic relations; presence of potentials does not restore localization at classical minima but reinforces bulk excitation.
AI.conclusion: Quantum effects in moduli spaces—governed by geometry and emergent taxonomy—naturally favor excited, positive-energy states localized away from classical expectations, suggesting a fundamental departure from semiclassical intuition in quantum gravity contexts.

authors: U. De Freitas Carneiro Da Graca
title: Improved branching-fraction measurements of $B^0_{(s)} \to K_S^0 h^+ h^{'-}$ decays and first observation of $B^0_(s) \to K_S^0 K^+ K^-$
comment: All figures and tables, along with any supplementary material and additional information, are available at https://lbfence.cern.ch/alcm/public/analysis/full-details/3621 (LHCb public pages)
summary:
This paper presents a study of the charmless three-body decays ${B^0_{(s)} \to K_{\mathrm{S}}^0 h^+ h^{\prime -}}$ (where $h^{(\prime)} = π, K$), using a sample of $pp$ collision data collected by the LHCb experiment, corresponding to an integrated luminosity of $9\mbox{\,fb}^{-1}$. The decay ${B^0_s \to K_{\mathrm{S}}^0 K^+ K^-}$ is observed for the first time, and the following ratios of branching fractions are measured: \begin{alignat*}{6}
  &\frac{{\cal B}(B^0 \to K^0_{\mathrm{S}} K^+ K^-)}{{\cal B}(B^0 \to K^0_{\mathrm{S}} π^+π^-)} &&= 0.578 &&\pm 0.007 &&\pm 0.017\,,
  &\frac{{\cal B}(B^0 \to K^0_{\mathrm{S}} K^\pmπ^\mp)}{{\cal B}(B^0 \to K^0_{\mathrm{S}} π^+π^-)} &&= 0.1363 &&\pm 0.0035 &&\pm 0.0051\,,
  &\frac{{\cal B}(B^0_s \to K^0_{\mathrm{S}} π^+π^-)}{{\cal B}(B^0 \to K^0_{\mathrm{S}} π^+π^-)} &&= 0.269 &&\pm 0.011 &&\pm 0.015 && \pm 0.008\,,
  &\frac{{\cal B}(B^0_s \to K^0_{\mathrm{S}} K^+ K^-)}{{\cal B}(B^0 \to K^0_{\mathrm{S}} π^+π^-)} &&= 0.0303 &&\pm 0.0041 &&\pm 0.0025 && \pm 0.0009\,,
  &\frac{{\cal B}(B^0_s \to K^0_{\mathrm{S}} K^\pmπ^\mp)}{{\cal B}(B^0 \to K^0_{\mathrm{S}} π^+π^-)} &&= 1.818 &&\pm 0.021 &&\pm 0.031 && \pm 0.056\,, \end{alignat*} where the uncertainties are statistical, systematic, and due to knowledge of the ratio of hadronisation fractions of the $B^0_s$ and $B^0$ mesons, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the first observation of the decay B⁰ₛ → K⁰ₛ K⁺K⁻ and measures several branching fraction ratios for charmless three-body B⁰_(s) → K⁰ₛ h⁺h′⁻ decays using 9 fb⁻¹ of LHCb pp collision data.
AI.motivation: To probe QCD dynamics and SU(3) flavor symmetry in charmless hadronic B decays, and to test theoretical predictions (e.g., factorization, QCD factorization, SCET) via precise measurements of branching fraction ratios across B⁰ and B⁰ₛ channels with different final-state meson compositions (ππ, KK, Kπ).
