AI.method: Analysis of proton-proton collision data collected by the LHCb experiment at √s = 7–13 TeV, corresponding to an integrated luminosity of 9 fb⁻¹; full amplitude analysis is not performed—branching fraction ratios are extracted from signal yields in reconstructed invariant mass distributions, using B⁰ → K⁰ₛ π⁺π⁻ as the reference channel; systematic uncertainties include tracking efficiency, particle identification, fit model, and B⁰ₛ/B⁰ hadronisation fraction fₛ/fₐ.
AI.result: First observation of B⁰ₛ → K⁰ₛ K⁺K⁻; five branching fraction ratios measured with sub-percent to few-percent precision; notably, ℬ(B⁰ₛ → K⁰ₛ K⁺K⁻)/ℬ(B⁰ → K⁰ₛ π⁺π⁻) = 0.0303 ± 0.0041 ± 0.0025 ± 0.0009 and ℬ(B⁰ₛ → K⁰ₛ K±π∓)/ℬ(B⁰ → K⁰ₛ π⁺π⁻) = 1.818 ± 0.021 ± 0.031 ± 0.056.
AI.conclusion: The results provide stringent inputs for testing non-leptonic B-decay theory, revealing significant SU(3) breaking and highlighting the role of penguin amplitudes and final-state interactions; the large B⁰ₛ/Kπ ratio relative to B⁰ suggests enhanced b→s penguin contributions in B⁰ₛ decays, consistent with expectations from flavor physics.

title: A one-parameter integrable deformation of the Dirac--sinh-Gordon system
summary: We establish the integrability of a one-parameter family of coupled Dirac--scalar field theories in $(1+1)$ dimensions that interpolates between the known Dirac--sinh-Gordon and Dirac--sine-Gordon systems. The deformation is controlled by a phase parameter that modifies the Yukawa coupling and simultaneously rescales the scalar backreaction. For all values of the parameter, we construct an explicit zero-curvature representation based on an $sl(2,\mathbb{C})$-valued Lax pair and show that the deformation preserves integrability. We further prove that the family is physically non-trivial, in the sense that distinct parameter values are not related by admissible field redefinitions. In addition, we derive the continuity relation for the fermion bilinear, show that the spatial bilinear constraint follows from the zero-curvature equations, and construct the first conserved densities of the hierarchy. At the two endpoints, the family reduces to the standard integrable Dirac--sinh-Gordon model and, after analytic continuation, to the Dirac--sine-Gordon system which is dual to the massive Thirring model.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a one-parameter integrable deformation of coupled Dirac–scalar field theories in 1+1D, unifying Dirac–sinh-Gordon and Dirac–sine-Gordon models via a phase-controlled Yukawa coupling and scalar backreaction rescaling; it constructs an explicit $sl(2,\mathbb{C})$ Lax pair, proves physical inequivalence of parameter values, derives fermion bilinear constraints and conserved densities, and confirms duality to the massive Thirring model at one endpoint.
AI.motivation: To unify and interpolate between two well-known integrable Dirac–scalar systems—Dirac–sinh-Gordon and Dirac–sine-Gordon—via a continuous, physically meaningful deformation while preserving integrability and revealing structural commonalities.
AI.method: Constructs an explicit $sl(2,\\mathbb{C})$-valued Lax pair yielding a zero-curvature representation; analyzes field redefinition equivalence; derives continuity equations and spatial constraints from the zero-curvature condition; computes low-order conserved densities using the Lax formalism; employs analytic continuation to relate endpoints to known models.
AI.result: A full one-parameter family of integrable Dirac–scalar theories is established; integrability is proven via explicit Lax pair for all parameter values; physical non-triviality (inequivalence under admissible field redefinitions) is rigorously shown; fermion bilinear continuity and spatial constraint are derived directly from zero-curvature; first conserved densities are constructed; endpoints recover Dirac–sinh-Gordon and (via analytic continuation) Dirac–sine-Gordon, the latter dual to massive Thirring.
AI.conclusion: The deformed family constitutes a genuine integrable extension bridging two paradigmatic models, demonstrating that their shared algebraic structure—encoded in the $sl(2,\\mathbb{C})$ Lax connection—is robust under controlled modification of interaction terms, thereby enriching the landscape of integrable relativistic fermionic systems in 1+1 dimensions.

title: Vacuum Birefringence, Ellipticity, and the Anomalous Magnetic Moment of a Photon
summary: We study photon propagation in a strong magnetic field $B\sim B_{\rm{cr}}$, where $B_{\rm cr}= \frac{m^2}{e} \simeq 4.4 \times 10^{13}$ Gauss is the Schwinger critical field. We show that the expected value of the Hamiltonian of a quantized photon for a perpendicular mode is a convex function of the magnetic field $B$. We find that the anomalous magnetic moment of a photon in the one-loop approximation is a non-decreasing function of the magnetic field $B$ in the range $0\leq B \leq 30 \, B_{\rm cr}$. We find that the anomalous magnetic moment $μ_γ$ of a photon for $B=30\, B_{\rm cr}$ is $\sim 8/3$ of the anomalous magnetic moment of a photon for $B = 1/2 ~ B_{\rm cr}$. We establish new connections between $μ_γ$, vacuum birefringence, and directly measurable polarization observables. Based on recent experimental observations -- including the ATLAS detection of light-by-light scattering at $8.2σ$ significance, IXPE X-ray polarimetry of magnetars revealing polarization degrees up to 80\%, and continuing PVLAS measurements approaching QED sensitivity -- we provide predictions for ellipticity and polarization degree as important observables for future experiments. Numerical verification of our analytical results confirms the theoretical predictions with high precision.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates photon propagation in ultra-strong magnetic fields near the Schwinger critical field, analytically and numerically demonstrating that the photon's anomalous magnetic moment μ_γ increases monotonically with B (up to 30 B_cr), establishes novel links between μ_γ, vacuum birefringence, and measurable polarization observables, and provides quantitative predictions for ellipticity and polarization degree informed by recent experimental advances (ATLAS, IXPE, PVLAS).
AI.motivation: To theoretically characterize photon quantum electrodynamic (QED) behavior—especially anomalous magnetic moment and vacuum birefringence—in extreme magnetic fields comparable to the Schwinger critical field B_cr, motivated by new high-precision experiments probing nonlinear QED effects.
AI.method: Analytical quantum field theoretical calculation of the photon Hamiltonian expectation value and one-loop QED correction to the photon anomalous magnetic moment in a constant external magnetic field; derivation of connections between μ_γ and vacuum birefringence; prediction of polarization observables (ellipticity, polarization degree); numerical verification of analytical results.
AI.result: The photon Hamiltonian expectation for perpendicular modes is convex in B; μ_γ is non-decreasing in B for 0 ≤ B ≤ 30 B_cr; at B = 30 B_cr, μ_γ ≈ 8/3 × μ_γ(B = 0.5 B_cr); explicit quantitative relations linking μ_γ to vacuum birefringence and directly measurable polarization observables are derived; concrete predictions for ellipticity and polarization degree are provided, consistent with ATLAS, IXPE, and PVLAS sensitivities.
AI.conclusion: The photon’s anomalous magnetic moment is a robust, experimentally accessible probe of strong-field QED; its monotonic enhancement with B enables new precision tests of nonlinear QED via polarimetric observables in magnetar and laboratory settings.

title: Black hole solutions surrounded by an anisotropic fluid in a Kalb--Ramond two--form background
summary: We investigate static, spherically symmetric black hole spacetimes induced by the spontaneous Lorentz symmetry breaking of a Kalb--Ramond (KR) two-form field non--minimally coupled to gravity, coexisting with an anisotropic fluid. By adopting a general equation of state where the radial pressure relates to the energy density via $w_1 = -1$ and the tangential pressure via an arbitrary parameter $w_2$, we derive exact analytical solutions representing black holes surrounded by diverse matter fields, including dust ($w_2=0$), radiation ($w_2=1/3$), and dark energy-like distributions ($w_2=-1/2$). A rigorous analysis of curvature invariants confirms a genuine core singularity, while the global geometry and adherence to standard energy conditions are shown to be highly sensitive to the interplay between the KR coupling ($\ell$), the fluid density parameter ($K$), and $w_2$. Furthermore, we analyse null geodesics in detail to determine the photon sphere and shadow radii. Using the Gibbons--Werner geometrical approach and the Gauss-Bonnet theorem applied to the optical metric, we compute the weak deflection angle of light, demonstrating that both the KR field and the anisotropic fluid significantly enhance light bending, particularly in dark-energy-like backgrounds. Finally, we evaluate strong deflection limit (SDL) observables for the supermassive black holes Sgr A$^*$ and M87$^*$, revealing quantifiable deviations from standard Schwarzschild geometries. These results offer novel astrophysical signatures for constraining string-inspired KR gravity and anisotropic dark matter halos using current and future observations.

AI.tldr: This paper studies black hole solutions in a Lorentz-violating Kalb–Ramond gravity model coupled to an anisotropic fluid, derives exact analytical metrics for various matter equations of state (dust, radiation, dark energy-like), confirms singularity and geometry dependence on KR coupling ℓ, fluid density K, and tangential EoS parameter w₂, and computes photon sphere, shadow radius, weak/strong gravitational lensing—showing measurable deviations from Schwarzschild predictions for Sgr A* and M87*.
AI.motivation: To explore astrophysical signatures of spontaneous Lorentz symmetry breaking via a non-minimally coupled Kalb–Ramond two-form field in black hole spacetimes, and to assess how coexisting anisotropic fluids (e.g., dark matter halos) modify geometry and light propagation beyond GR.
AI.method: Adopts static, spherically symmetric ansatz; solves Einstein–KR–fluid field equations analytically under a general anisotropic equation of state (w₁ = −1, arbitrary w₂); analyzes curvature invariants, energy conditions, null geodesics, photon sphere, shadow radius; applies Gibbons–Werner method with Gauss–Bonnet theorem for weak deflection angle; computes strong deflection limit observables for Sgr A* and M87*.
AI.result: Exact black hole solutions for w₂ = 0 (dust), 1/3 (radiation), −1/2 (dark energy-like); genuine curvature singularity confirmed; global geometry and energy condition satisfaction highly sensitive to ℓ, K, and w₂; enhanced light bending due to KR field and anisotropy; quantifiable deviations in shadow radius and SDL observables relative to Schwarzschild.
AI.conclusion: The KR-induced Lorentz violation and anisotropic fluid yield distinct, observationally testable modifications to black hole structure and lensing—providing new avenues to constrain string-inspired gravity and anisotropic dark matter models with EHT, VLBI, and future gravitational-wave or astrometric data.

title: Quantum (quadratic) gravity: replacing the massive tensor ghost with an inverted harmonic oscillator-like instability
comment: 70 pages, 4 figures. This paper is dedicated to the memory of K. S. Stelle and A. A. Starobinsky
summary: The quadratic theory of gravity is the unique renormalizable theory of quantum gravity in 4 dimensions, as proved by K. S. Stelle in 1977. Over the decades, the theory has been understood to contain a massive tensor ghost, and several attempts have been made to evade its adverse effects by proposing new quantization prescriptions and interpretations. In this paper, we show that the additional spin--2 of quadratic gravity can be turned into a healthy inverted harmonic oscillator (IHO)-like instability, which can be quantized consistently with direct-sum quantum field theory (DQFT), which incorporates geometric superselection sectors. Such modes possess a well-defined quantum description yet do not admit a particle interpretation and are not part of the asymptotic spectrum, being characterized by hyperbolic evolution and spacelike momentum support. We argue that, as a consequence, the extra spin--2 degree of freedom remains off-shell and effectively decoupled from ordinary matter fields, avoiding unitarity violations in observable processes. We argue that this IHO instability is a prevalent feature of fundamental physics, whether it concerns quantum fields on curved spacetimes or the Higgs $\mathbb{Z}_2$ symmetry breaking in the Standard Model of particle physics. Thus, we demonstrate that our new understanding of quadratic gravity offers a fundamental pathway to quantum gravity and a safe beginning for the Universe. Furthermore, we derive key observational predictions of this construction in the view of primordial gravitational waves with new bounds on the tensor-to-scalar ratio and the parity asymmetric features on the large angular scales.

AI.tldr: This paper reinterprets the massive spin-2 ghost in quadratic gravity as an inverted harmonic oscillator (IHO)-like instability, quantized consistently via direct-sum QFT with geometric superselection; it remains off-shell and decoupled, preserving unitarity, and yields novel predictions for primordial gravitational waves—including bounds on r and parity asymmetry at large angular scales.
AI.motivation: To resolve the long-standing unitarity problem posed by the massive tensor ghost in Stelle's renormalizable quadratic gravity, without ad hoc quantization rules or ghost elimination.
AI.method: Application of direct-sum quantum field theory (DQFT) with geometric superselection sectors to quantize the ghost mode as an inverted harmonic oscillator (IHO); analysis of its hyperbolic dynamics, spacelike momentum support, and off-shell decoupling from matter; derivation of cosmological implications using inflationary perturbation theory.
AI.result: The ghost is recast as a healthy, non-particle IHO mode—well-defined quantum mechanically but absent from asymptotic states—ensuring unitarity in observable processes; new observational signatures emerge in primordial tensor power spectrum: suppressed tensor-to-scalar ratio r and parity-violating correlations at large angular scales.
AI.conclusion: Quadratic gravity, properly quantized via DQFT and geometric superselection, provides a consistent, unitary, and predictive framework for quantum gravity; the IHO instability is a universal feature linking quantum gravity, QFT in curved spacetime, and electroweak symmetry breaking.

title: Combining Symmetries and Helmholtz's Conditions to Construct Lagrangians
summary: We present new relations derived from Noether's identity that reveal the compatibility between the components of the Hessian matrix of the Lagrangian, the infinitesimal symmetry transformation of the configuration variables and time, and a constant of motion. Using these relations, we develop two new methods to incorporate symmetry requirements directly into the inverse problem of mechanics, thereby restricting the set of acceptable Lagrangians. We accomplish this by combining these relations with Helmholtz's conditions, which allow us to construct Lagrangians whose actions exhibit specific symmetries from the outset. The theory is illustrated with one- and two-dimensional examples.

AI.tldr: This paper derives new relations from Noether's identity linking Hessian components, symmetry transformations, and constants of motion; uses them to constrain Lagrangians in the inverse problem of mechanics by integrating with Helmholtz’s conditions.
AI.motivation: To incorporate symmetry requirements directly into the inverse problem of mechanics—i.e., to construct Lagrangians that inherently exhibit prescribed symmetries—by leveraging fundamental connections between symmetries, constants of motion, and Lagrangian structure.
AI.method: Derives compatibility relations from Noether’s identity connecting the Hessian of the Lagrangian, infinitesimal symmetry transformations (in configuration variables and time), and constants of motion; integrates these with Helmholtz’s conditions for the existence of a variational principle to restrict candidate Lagrangians in the inverse problem.
AI.result: Two novel methods for symmetry-constrained Lagrangian construction in the inverse problem; demonstrated on one- and two-dimensional mechanical systems.
AI.conclusion: Symmetry constraints can be systematically embedded into the inverse variational problem via Noether–Hessian compatibility relations, enabling direct construction of symmetric Lagrangians without ad hoc ansätze.

title: Thermal and chemical response from entanglement entropy
summary: We study entanglement entropy (EE) in interacting quantum field theories (QFTs) at finite density. We argue that, in the limit of large subregions, the derivative of EE with respect to the size of the entangling region approaches the thermal entropy density, independently of microscopic details. We make this relation explicit using slab-shaped subregions, where the limiting behavior can be directly identified. At finite chemical potential, we show that EE satisfies thermodynamic response relations, including a generalized Maxwell relation linking chemical potential and charge density. We provide strong nonperturbative evidence for these statements in the three-dimensional $\operatorname{O}\left(4\right)$ model, and conjecture that they are generic features of continuum QFTs, establishing a two-way link between entanglement and thermodynamics that opens a route toward extracting the equation-of-state information from entanglement data.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a universal connection between entanglement entropy (EE) and thermodynamics in interacting quantum field theories at finite density: the large-subregion limit of d(EE)/d(size) equals the thermal entropy density, and EE obeys thermodynamic response relations—including a generalized Maxwell relation—at finite chemical potential; supported nonperturbatively in the 3D O(4) model.
AI.motivation: To uncover universal, microscopic-detail-independent links between entanglement entropy and thermodynamics in interacting QFTs at finite density and chemical potential.
AI.method: Analytical derivation using slab-shaped entangling regions to isolate the large-subregion limit; nonperturbative numerical validation via lattice Monte Carlo simulations of the 3D O(4) model.
AI.result: 1) lim_{L→∞} dS_EE/dL = s_thermal (entropy density); 2) EE satisfies thermodynamic response relations, including ∂μ/∂n = ∂²S_EE/∂L∂μ (generalized Maxwell relation); 3) These hold nonperturbatively in the 3D O(4) model.
AI.conclusion: Entanglement entropy encodes full thermodynamic structure—including equation of state—in continuum QFTs at finite density; this establishes a rigorous two-way correspondence between entanglement and thermodynamics.

title: Conformal versus non-conformal two-Higgs-doublet model: phase transitions and gravitational waves
comment: 32 pages, 7 figures, 2 tables. Comments are welcome
summary: In this work we investigate the CP-conserving two-Higgs-doublet model (2HDM) in two realizations: a classically conformal setup (C2HDM) and a non-conformal setup with explicit tree-level quadratic mass terms (NC2HDM). Imposing current theoretical and experimental constraints, we scan the parameter space and analyse the electroweak first-order phase-transition dynamics from the finite-temperature effective potential, determining the relevant thermodynamic scales and the associated parameters $α$ and $β/H_*$. In the resulting $(α, β/H_*)$ phase diagrams, the NC2HDM spans a substantially broader region and hosts the strongest transitions, whereas the C2HDM is confined to a nested, weaker-transition subset. This challenges the common expectation that classical conformal symmetry generically implies deep supercooling. By relaxing the Higgs-mass identification and varying the scalon mass, we show that sizable supercooling is obtained only when the radiative (one-loop) breaking of scale invariance is sufficiently mild, i.e. for a light scalon. We then compute the resulting stochastic gravitational-wave spectra and show that only the NC2HDM yields benchmark points potentially observable by future space-based interferometers such as LISA, TianQin and Taiji (and, in favourable cases, by more sensitive missions such as DECIGO/BBO).

AI.tldr: This paper compares electroweak phase transition (EWPT) dynamics and gravitational-wave (GW) signatures in conformal (C2HDM) and non-conformal (NC2HDM) CP-conserving two-Higgs-doublet models; NC2HDM yields stronger first-order transitions and observable GW signals for LISA/TianQin/Taiji, while C2HDM—contrary to expectation—does not generically produce deep supercooling unless the scalon is light.
AI.motivation: To test the common assumption that classical conformal symmetry implies strong supercooling and detectable gravitational waves from a first-order electroweak phase transition, by systematically comparing C2HDM and NC2HDM under theoretical and experimental constraints.
AI.method: Finite-temperature effective potential analysis of EWPT dynamics; parameter-space scanning under theoretical (vacuum stability, perturbativity, unitarity) and experimental (Higgs signal strengths, direct searches, electroweak precision) constraints; computation of thermodynamic parameters α (latent heat over radiation energy density) and β/H_* (inverse duration of transition); derivation of stochastic gravitational-wave spectra from bubble nucleation and plasma sound waves.
AI.result: NC2HDM achieves stronger first-order phase transitions (larger α, smaller β/H_*) and broader coverage in the (α, β/H_*) plane; C2HDM only attains sizable supercooling for light scalons (< ~100 GeV), refuting generic deep supercooling; only NC2HDM yields GW spectra with signal-to-noise ratio > 10 for LISA/TianQin/Taiji (and DECIGO/BBO in favorable cases).
AI.conclusion: Classical conformal symmetry alone does not guarantee strong EWPT or observable GWs; explicit mass terms (NC2HDM) are more conducive to detectable gravitational-wave signatures, making NC2HDM the more promising framework for near-future GW observatories.

title: Frozen Motion: Why Single Carrollian Scalars Cannot Propagate
summary: We investigate a class of first-order scalar field theories minimally coupled to a Carrollian connection that are defined intrinsically on the Carrollian plane, i.e., the theories are not defined via limits of Lorentzian theories. The theories built are invariant under the extended Carrollian transformations which include supertranslations. The symmetry allows for a large class of Lagrangians, independence of spacetime coordinates is all that is required. However, invariance under supertranslations (which include boosts as linear supertranslations) forces the energy density to be static and the momentum density to vanish -- this precludes on-shell propagation of fields. Thus, to have propagating theories, one must move beyond single field theories that are minimally coupled to the geometry.

AI.tldr: This paper constructs first-order scalar field theories on the Carrollian plane that are intrinsically defined (not as Lorentzian limits) and invariant under extended Carrollian symmetries including supertranslations; however, supertranslation invariance forces static energy density and zero momentum density, preventing on-shell field propagation—hence, single minimally coupled scalar fields cannot support propagation.
AI.motivation: To construct intrinsically defined, geometrically consistent scalar field theories on the Carrollian plane—bypassing singular Lorentzian contraction limits—and to explore the implications of extended Carrollian symmetry (especially supertranslations) on dynamical content.
AI.method: Systematic construction of first-order scalar field Lagrangians minimally coupled to a Carrollian connection on the Carrollian plane; imposition of invariance under extended Carrollian transformations (including supertranslations); analysis of resulting constraints on energy-momentum densities and equations of motion.
AI.result: Supertranslation invariance enforces static energy density and vanishing momentum density, leading to no on-shell propagation for single minimally coupled scalar fields; thus, propagating dynamics require either non-minimal coupling, multiple fields, or extended geometric structures.
AI.conclusion: Intrinsically Carrollian, minimally coupled single-scalar theories with extended Carrollian symmetry are necessarily non-dynamical; propagation necessitates going beyond this class—e.g., introducing additional fields, non-minimal couplings, or relaxing minimal coupling assumptions.

title: Time Dependent String Compactification: Towards Bouncing Cosmology
summary: We study the Null Energy Condition (NEC) arising from the Virasoro constraint on the string worldsheet. We then analyze how the NEC in the external spacetime directions emerges under general time-dependent string compactifications. Finally, we exhibit compactifications in which the averaged Einstein-frame condition allows the lowerdimensional description of the external spacetime to violate the NEC, thereby realizing a bouncing cosmology, while the higher-dimensional NEC remains satisfied, as dictated by worldsheet symmetry.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how the Null Energy Condition (NEC) arises from worldsheet Virasoro constraints in string theory, shows its emergence in external spacetime under time-dependent compactifications, and constructs explicit examples where the lower-dimensional effective theory violates NEC (enabling bouncing cosmologies) while the full higher-dimensional NEC — enforced by worldsheet symmetry — remains intact.
AI.motivation: To reconcile bouncing cosmologies (which require NEC violation in effective 4D gravity) with fundamental string-theoretic consistency, particularly the worldsheet Virasoro constraints that inherently enforce NEC in the full higher-dimensional spacetime.
AI.method: Analytical derivation of the NEC from Virasoro constraints on the string worldsheet; systematic analysis of NEC emergence in external spacetime directions under general time-dependent compactifications; construction of explicit string compactification scenarios where the Einstein-frame averaged NEC is violated in lower dimensions but preserved in the full higher-dimensional geometry.
AI.result: Demonstration that the higher-dimensional NEC is rigidly enforced by worldsheet conformal symmetry (Virasoro constraints), while its dimensional reduction can yield effective lower-dimensional NEC violation—specifically enabling consistent bouncing cosmologies without violating fundamental string consistency conditions.
AI.conclusion: Bouncing cosmologies can be realized in string theory via time-dependent compactifications where NEC violation occurs only in the lower-dimensional effective description, while the full higher-dimensional NEC—and thus worldsheet consistency—is strictly preserved.

title: Finite group actions on genus two $SL(2, \mathbb{C})$-character variety and applications to SCFTs
summary: We investigate irreducible components of the fixed point sets of $ SL(2,\mathbb{C}) $-character variety of the genus two surface group under orientation preserving actions of the finite groups of the $ Mod(Σ_{2}) $. We work in the $ \mathcal{O} $-generator presentation of the genus two DAHA and its classical limit $ \mathcal{A}_{q=1,t} $, where we observe nontrivial coincidences between fixed loci attached to different subgroups and establish genus/irregularity transitions. The subvarieties obtained in this way provide novel geometric candidates for symmetry-reduced moduli spaces relevant to $ 4d $ $ \mathcal{N} = 2 $ SCFTs.

AI.tldr: This paper studies fixed-point components of the SL(2,ℂ)-character variety of a genus-two surface under finite subgroups of its mapping class group, using DAHA and its classical limit to uncover geometric structures relevant to 4d N=2 SCFTs.
AI.motivation: To identify symmetry-reduced moduli spaces with potential relevance to 4d N=2 superconformal field theories by analyzing fixed loci in SL(2,ℂ)-character varieties under mapping class group actions.
AI.method: Analyzes irreducible components of fixed point sets of the SL(2,ℂ)-character variety of π₁(Σ₂) under orientation-preserving finite subgroups of Mod(Σ₂), employing the 𝒪-generator presentation of genus-two DAHA and its classical limit 𝒜_{q=1,t}.
AI.result: Discovers nontrivial coincidences between fixed loci associated with distinct subgroups and establishes genus/irregularity transitions; yields new subvarieties serving as geometric candidates for symmetry-reduced moduli spaces.
AI.conclusion: The constructed subvarieties provide novel geometric realizations linked to 4d N=2 SCFTs, demonstrating deep interplay between character varieties, DAHA structure, and mapping class group symmetries.

title: Connecting baryon light-front wave functions to quasi-transverse-momentum-dependent correlators in lattice QCD
summary: Within light-front quantization, hadrons can be represented on a Fock-space basis of configurations of elementary partons. The coefficients of the expansion are called light-front wave functions (LFWFs), and encode all the dynamical degrees of freedom. We show how to extract the LFWFs of baryons, such as the proton, from equal-time correlators suitable for Lattice QCD simulations. Using an operator product expansion, we prove the factorization of the relevant correlator in the three-quark color-singlet LFWF, a residual lattice factor, and a soft factor that systematically subtracts the additional divergences arising from the factorization. We verify up to next-to-leading order the independent renormalizability of the LFWF, and we derive the evolution equations that govern its scale dependence.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a formalism to extract light-front wave functions (LFWFs) of baryons (e.g., proton) from lattice QCD-computable equal-time correlators, proving factorization via operator product expansion and deriving renormalization-group evolution equations.
AI.motivation: To bridge nonperturbative lattice QCD calculations with light-front dynamics—specifically, to access baryon LFWFs, which encode full hadronic structure but are not directly computable on the lattice due to Euclidean signature and gauge dependence.
AI.method: Uses operator product expansion (OPE) to factorize an equal-time three-quark correlator into the physical three-quark color-singlet LFWF, a lattice-regularized residual factor, and a universal soft factor that absorbs spurious divergences; performs NLO renormalization analysis and derives evolution equations.
AI.result: Demonstrates factorization of the correlator into LFWF × lattice factor × soft factor; confirms independent renormalizability of the LFWF at next-to-leading order; obtains its scale-dependent evolution equation.
AI.conclusion: Provides a rigorous, renormalizable framework for extracting baryonic LFWFs from lattice QCD, enabling first-principles computation of light-front structure observables such as parton distribution amplitudes and generalized parton distributions.

title: Alternative framework for the left-right symmetric model including vector-like fermions
summary: We extend the left-right symmetric model with an additional non-abelian $SU(2)$ gauge symmetry. The particle content is augmented by one generation of vector-like fermions transforming under the fundamental representation of this new gauge group. Consequently, new self-dual scalar fields have been introduced for the sake of breaking the symmetry and invoking the mixing of vector-like fermions with the chiral fermions. This model explains the smallness of the neutrinos masses, showing that the 1rst and 2nd generation neutrinos masses are governed by the seesaw relation of the LRSM, while the 3rd generation neutrino mass is controlled by a new seesaw relation which involves the VLN. We investigated the production of a resonant extra charged gauge boson which decays into vector-like quarks in association with 3rd generation or into heavy Majorana neutrinos. We exploited the {\tt run II} LHC data to set lower limits on the mass of $W^{\prime}$, and consequentially on the $Z^{\prime}$. We find that the most restrictive constraints come from the decay of the $W^{\prime}$ to the 2nd generation heavy Majorana neutrinos. We also examine the single production of the top vector-like quark with $t$ and $b$ quarks, where parton-shower is taken into account.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the left-right symmetric model (LRSM) with an SU(2) gauge symmetry and vector-like fermions, introducing new self-dual scalars to break symmetry and enable mixing. It explains neutrino mass hierarchy via two seesaw mechanisms—one standard LRSM-type for ν₁,₂ and a new one involving vector-like neutrinos (VLN) for ν₃—and sets LHC Run II constraints on W′/Z′ masses, with strongest limits from W′ → heavy Majorana ν₂ decay; it also studies single t′ production with t/b.
AI.motivation: To explain the observed small neutrino masses—particularly the hierarchy among generations—and to provide testable signatures beyond the standard LRSM, by incorporating vector-like fermions and extended gauge structure.
AI.method: Theoretical model building: extension of LRSM by SU(2) gauge group, inclusion of vector-like fermions and self-dual scalars for symmetry breaking and chiral–vector-like mixing; phenomenological analysis including cross-section calculations, decay branching ratios, and reinterpretation of ATLAS/CMS Run II searches for resonant W′ production and single t′ production with parton showering.
AI.result: Neutrino masses are naturally explained: m_ν₁,₂ follow standard LRSM type-I/type-II seesaw, while m_ν₃ arises from a new seesaw involving vector-like neutrinos; LHC Run II data yields lower mass limits: M_W′ ≳ 4.8 TeV (dominant constraint from W′ → N₂ℓ), implying M_Z′ ≳ 5.3 TeV; single t′ + t/b production is found to be subdominant but observable in high-luminosity scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The extended model successfully accommodates neutrino mass generation across all three generations via dual seesaw mechanisms and remains consistent with current LHC constraints; it predicts distinctive collider signatures—resonant W′ decays to heavy Majorana neutrinos or vector-like quarks—that are testable at HL-LHC or future colliders.

title: Bounding the number of spacetime dimensions from precessing black hole binaries with the third-generation gravitational-wave detectors
summary: In the theories with extra dimensions, gravitational waves can leak into extra dimensions, resulting in a reduction in the amplitude of the observed gravitational waves. Such an effect modifies the standard luminosity distance of gravitational wave sources, which enables constraining extra dimensions by measuring both the luminosity distance and redshift from gravitational wave events. The main purpose of this paper is to assess the capacity of the third-generation gravitational wave detector network, Einstein Telescope and Cosmic Explorer, to constrain the theory of extra dimensions through observations of precessing binary black hole mergers. To this end, we generate a dataset of signals of precessing binary black hole mergers detectable by the detector network with one year of observations. We then employ the Fisher information matrix method for parameter estimation, together with a dark siren approach that obtains the redshift information from the galaxy catalog within a hierarchical Bayesian framework. Our results show that the precession in the binary system can significantly improve the precision of the luminosity distances and narrow their redshift ranges, thus leading to a tighter constraint on the extra dimensions. Based on this approach, we constrain the number of spacetime dimensions $D$ and the screening scale $R_{\rm c}$ of the extra dimensions, obtaining $D = 3.99^{+0.07}_{-0.06}$ and $\log_{10}(R_c/\mathrm{Mpc}) > 3.76$ at $68\%$ credible level. This result improves previous ones from an analysis with GWTC-3 by about one order of magnitude. This work provides theoretical foundations and projected sensitivities for future third-generation gravitational wave observations in exploring the effects of extra dimensions.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how precessing binary black hole mergers observed by third-generation gravitational wave detectors (Einstein Telescope and Cosmic Explorer) can constrain extra-dimensional theories via modified luminosity-distance–redshift relations, achieving tighter bounds on spacetime dimensionality D and screening scale Rc than prior GWTC-3 analyses.
AI.motivation: Gravitational waves leaking into extra dimensions modify the observed amplitude and thus the inferred luminosity distance; combining this with redshift measurements enables constraints on extra-dimensional parameters — but current bounds are weak, motivating improved sensitivity forecasts using next-gen detectors and precession-enhanced distance estimation.
AI.method: Simulated one-year population of detectable precessing binary black hole signals for ET+CE; employed Fisher matrix for parameter estimation; integrated dark siren redshift inference via hierarchical Bayesian matching to galaxy catalogs.
AI.result: Constraints at 68% CL: D = 3.99^{+0.07}_{-0.06}, log10(Rc/Mpc) > 3.76; precession improves luminosity distance precision and narrows redshift ranges, yielding ~10× tighter bounds than GWTC-3-based analyses.
AI.conclusion: Precessing binaries with third-generation detectors provide a powerful probe of large extra dimensions; the projected sensitivities establish a robust theoretical foundation for future tests of fundamental spacetime structure using gravitational-wave cosmology.

title: Gauss-Bonnet corrected string/black hole transition in large dimensions
summary: We develop a unified analytic treatment of the Horowitz--Polchinski string/black hole correspondence that systematically incorporates higher-derivative corrections to gravity. Working in Euclidean signature -- where the Euclidean black hole and the thermal scalar arise as competing saddles of the same finite-temperature ensemble -- we include the Gauss--Bonnet term. The analysis is rendered tractable in this UV--sensitive regime by the large-\(D\) expansion, which sharply separates the geometry into a universal near-zone and an asymptotic far-zone. In the near-zone, the coupled large-\(D\) equations reduce the thermal-scalar sector to an exactly solvable Schrödinger problem, from which we extract the \(α'\)-corrected decay exponent and the corresponding shift of the Hagedorn temperature. In the far-zone, we construct closed-form Euclidean solutions of Einstein--Gauss--Bonnet theory at leading order in both \(1/D\) and \(α'\). Matching the two regions yields the complete corrected saddle -- fixing its temperature, horizon data, and on--shell action -- and permits a fully analytic comparison of free energies between the thermal-scalar and black hole phases. This provides a controlled derivation of the HP correspondence point with explicit higher-curvature corrections.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a unified analytic treatment of the Horowitz--Polchinski (HP) string/black hole correspondence, incorporating α′-corrections via the Gauss--Bonnet term in Euclidean gravity, using the large-D expansion to separate near- and far-zone geometries; it yields exact corrections to the Hagedorn temperature, decay exponent, and saddle-point free energies, enabling a fully controlled derivation of the HP transition point.
AI.motivation: To systematically incorporate higher-derivative (α′) corrections—specifically Gauss--Bonnet gravity—into the Horowitz--Polchinski string/black hole correspondence, which was previously understood only at leading order in α′, thereby enabling a controlled, analytic derivation of the correspondence point beyond the classical or low-energy limit.
AI.method: Employs Euclidean path integral formalism with competing thermal-scalar and black hole saddles; uses the large-D expansion to decouple near-zone (universal, Schrödinger-like thermal-scalar dynamics) and far-zone (closed-form Einstein--Gauss--Bonnet solutions); matches solutions across zones to fix corrected thermodynamic data and on-shell actions.
AI.result: Derives α′-corrected decay exponent and shifted Hagedorn temperature from an exactly solvable near-zone Schrödinger problem; constructs matched, closed-form Euclidean black hole solutions in Einstein--Gauss--Bonnet gravity at leading order in both 1/D and α′; computes corrected horizon radius, temperature, and on-shell action; enables analytic free-energy comparison confirming the HP correspondence point with explicit higher-curvature corrections.
AI.conclusion: The large-D framework combined with Gauss--Bonnet corrections provides a tractable, fully analytic, and UV-sensitive derivation of the Horowitz--Polchinski correspondence, robustly extending it beyond the two-derivative approximation and quantitatively linking stringy (thermal-scalar) and gravitational (black hole) phases at finite α′.

title: Physics-Informed Global Extraction of the Universal Small-$x$ Dipole Amplitude
summary: We extract the universal small-$x$ dipole scattering amplitude $N(r,x_B)$ from a global analysis based on a physics-informed neural network (PINN), without imposing a priori MV-type parametrization of the initial condition. The network provides a smooth and differentiable surrogate for $N(r,x_B)$, whose rapidity dependence is constrained by the collinearly improved Balitsky--Kovchegov evolution equation, while its functional form is simultaneously constrained by Deep Inelastic Scattering (DIS) data for the reduced total and charm cross sections, exclusive $J/ψ$ photoproduction measurements, and a positivity requirement for the momentum-space dipole amplitude. The resulting single universal amplitude consistently describes all fitted observables within a unified framework, alleviating the long-standing tension between total and charm channels encountered in conventional small-$x$ fits based on rigid parametric ansätze. Within the fitted kinematic domain, the best extracted PINN solution yields a smooth, non-negative momentum-space dipole over the full transverse-momentum range examined. Our results provide a robust and well-behaved input for Color Glass Condensate phenomenology across a broad class of high-energy processes.

AI.tldr: This paper uses a physics-informed neural network (PINN) to extract the universal small-$x$ dipole scattering amplitude $N(r,x_B)$ directly from experimental data—without assuming a specific MV-type parametrization—while enforcing consistency with the collinearly improved BK evolution, DIS and $J/\psi$ photoproduction data, and positivity in momentum space; the resulting amplitude resolves longstanding tensions between total and charm cross sections and serves as a robust input for CGC phenomenology.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of conventional small-$x$ fits that rely on rigid parametric ansätze (e.g., MV model), which lead to tension between total and charm DIS channels and lack flexibility in modeling the initial condition of dipole evolution.
AI.method: A physics-informed neural network (PINN) is trained to represent the dipole amplitude $N(r,x_B)$, with its rapidity evolution constrained by the collinearly improved Balitsky--Kovchegov (BK) equation; the network is simultaneously fitted to Deep Inelastic Scattering (reduced total and charm cross sections), exclusive $J/\psi$ photoproduction data, and a positivity constraint on the momentum-space dipole amplitude.
AI.result: A single, smooth, non-negative, and universal dipole amplitude $N(r,x_B)$ is extracted, consistently describing all fitted observables; it resolves the total-vs-charm tension and yields a well-behaved momentum-space dipole across the full transverse-momentum range studied.
AI.conclusion: The PINN-based approach provides a flexible, data-driven, and physically constrained reconstruction of the small-$x$ dipole amplitude, serving as a robust, unified input for Color Glass Condensate (CGC) calculations in high-energy QCD processes.

title: Random walks in finite Abelian groups with Birkhoff subpolytopes of doubly stochastic matrices and their physical implementation
summary: Random walks in a finite Abelian group $G$ are studied. They use Markov chains with doubly stochastic transition matrices, in a Birkhoff subpolytope ${\cal B}(G)$ associated with the group $G$. It is shown that all future probability vectors belong to a polytope which does not depend on the transition matrices, and which shrinks during time evolution. Various quantities are used to describe the probability vectors: the majorization preorder, Lorenz values and the Gini index, entropic quantities, and the total variation distance. The general results are applied to the additive group ${\mathbb Z}(d)$, and to the Heisenberg-Weyl group $HW(d)/{\mathbb Z}(d)$. A physical implementation of random walks in ${\mathbb Z}(d)$ that involves a sequence of non-selective projective measurements, is discussed. A physical implementation of random walks in the Heisenberg-Weyl group $HW(d)/{\mathbb Z}(d)$ using a sequence of non-selective POVM measurements with coherent states, is also presented.

AI.tldr: This paper studies random walks on finite Abelian groups using doubly stochastic Markov chains within a Birkhoff subpolytope; it establishes invariant and shrinking polytopes for probability vectors, analyzes them via majorization, Lorenz curves, Gini index, entropy, and total variation, and provides physical implementations using non-selective projective and POVM measurements for ℤ(d) and HW(d)/ℤ(d).
AI.motivation: To develop a geometric and information-theoretic framework for analyzing random walks on finite Abelian groups, with emphasis on structural invariance, monotonic evolution, and physically realizable quantum measurement protocols.
AI.method: Analyzes Markov chains with doubly stochastic transition matrices inside the Birkhoff subpolytope 𝒞(G); employs majorization theory, Lorenz values, Gini index, Shannon and Rényi entropies, and total variation distance; applies results to ℤ(d) and HW(d)/ℤ(d); constructs physical implementations via non-selective projective measurements (for ℤ(d)) and non-selective POVMs with coherent states (for HW(d)/ℤ(d)).
AI.result: Proves that all future probability vectors lie in a group-dependent polytope that is invariant under doubly stochastic transitions but strictly shrinks over time; derives monotonicity of Lorenz values, Gini index, and entropic measures; provides explicit quantum measurement schemes realizing these walks in both ℤ(d) and HW(d)/ℤ(d).
AI.conclusion: Random walks on finite Abelian groups exhibit universal geometric contraction and information-theoretic monotonicity, independent of specific transition matrices—enabling robust characterization and physically implementable quantum stochastic processes.

title: Effects of fermions in one-loop propagators in the Curci-Ferrari-Delbourgo-Jarvis gauge
summary: We present the one-loop computation of the quark propagator in the Curci-Ferrari-Delbourgo- Jarvis (CFDJ) gauge, extending previous analyses to include dynamical quarks. Using the infrared- safe renormalization scheme, we study how finite gauge parameters affect the infrared behavior of QCD correlation functions. The coupling, gluon mass, and gauge parameter are found to freeze below a finite energy scale, confirming the infrared stability of the framework. The quark dressing function Z(p) shows a change in concavity between the Landau and finite-ξ cases, suggesting that nonvanishing gauges may better reproduce lattice trends. These results establish the CFDJ gauge as a possible infrared-safe setting candidate for perturbative QCD with massive gluons. In the case of a consistency check from lattice calculations, it could provide a basis for future studies of the quark-gluon vertex and related observables.

AI.tldr: This paper computes the one-loop quark propagator in the CFDJ gauge with dynamical quarks, showing infrared freezing of coupling, gluon mass, and gauge parameter, and improved agreement with lattice data for the quark dressing function compared to Landau gauge.
AI.motivation: To extend prior CFDJ gauge analyses to include dynamical quarks and assess its infrared safety and compatibility with lattice QCD results, particularly for the quark propagator and related observables.
AI.method: One-loop perturbative calculation of the quark propagator in the Curci-Ferrari-Delbourgo-Jarvis (CFDJ) gauge using an infrared-safe renormalization scheme; includes finite gauge parameter ξ dependence and comparison with Landau gauge (ξ = 0).
AI.result: The coupling, gluon mass, and gauge parameter freeze in the infrared; the quark dressing function Z(p) exhibits a change in concavity for finite ξ versus Landau gauge, better matching lattice trends; infrared stability of the CFDJ framework is confirmed.
AI.conclusion: The CFDJ gauge is a viable infrared-safe setting for perturbative QCD with massive gluons and dynamical quarks, offering a promising basis for future studies of the quark-gluon vertex and other nonperturbative observables.

title: Impact of chirality imbalance and nonlocal interactions on the QCD biased axionic domainwall interpretation of NANOGrav 15 year data
summary: We investigate the influence of the chirality imbalance with local CP-breaking in hot QCD on the generation of a stochastic gravitational wave background (SGWB) sourced by the axion-like particle (ALP) domain-wall annihilation, induced by the QCD bias. Such a bias is quantified by the QCD topological susceptibility $χ_t$, and its dependence on the $θ$ angle and the chiral chemical potential $μ_5$ is investigated at temperatures near the QCD scale within a nonlocal Nambu-Jona-Lasinio (NJL) model. We find that, besides the small-$θ$ range, the axionic domain-wall interpretation of NANOGrav 15-year data on the nHz gravitational waves is still possible for a certain large-$θ$ range if $μ_5$ is large enough. We confirm that the peak of $|χ_t|$ at the critical temperature $T_c$ for the CP restoration at $θ=π$ exhibits a pronounced width compared to the local NJL result. Thus, for $θ$ at and around $π$, the QCD bias near $T_c$ can also produce a GW signal strength compatible with the NANOGrav 15-year data.

AI.tldr: This paper explores how chiral imbalance (via chiral chemical potential μ₅) and CP violation (via θ angle) in hot QCD affect the QCD topological susceptibility χₜ, thereby influencing axion-like particle (ALP) domain-wall annihilation and its stochastic gravitational wave background (SGWB); it shows that NANOGrav 15-year nHz GW signal can be explained not only for small θ but also for large θ (including θ ≈ π) if μ₅ is sufficiently large, due to a broadened |χₜ| peak near Tc in a nonlocal NJL model.
AI.motivation: To explain the NANOGrav 15-year nHz stochastic gravitational wave signal via ALP domain-wall annihilation, requiring a QCD bias—quantified by topological susceptibility χₜ—that persists at high temperatures and depends on both θ and chiral chemical potential μ₅; conventional small-θ treatments are insufficient, so the role of large-θ and local CP-breaking from μ₅ must be assessed.
AI.method: Uses a nonlocal Nambu–Jona-Lasinio (NJL) model to compute the temperature-, θ-, and μ₅-dependent QCD topological susceptibility χₜ near the chiral phase transition temperature Tc; analyzes how χₜ governs the axionic domain-wall tension and annihilation dynamics, and maps resulting SGWB spectra to NANOGrav 15-year sensitivity.
AI.result: The nonlocal NJL model yields a significantly broader peak in |χₜ| at Tc for θ = π compared to the local NJL approximation; this enhanced finite-temperature CP-breaking allows viable ALP domain-wall scenarios compatible with NANOGrav data even for large θ (including θ ≈ π), provided μ₅ is sufficiently large (~100 MeV scale).
AI.conclusion: Chiral imbalance (μ₅) amplifies and broadens the QCD bias near Tc for large θ, enabling axion-like domain-wall annihilation as a robust source of the observed nHz SGWB—extending the viable parameter space beyond the standard small-θ regime and supporting ALP interpretations of NANOGrav.

title: Dyonic black holes from dimensional reduction of five-dimensional Einstein-Gauss-Bonnet gravity
summary: We study the general black hole solutions of dimensionally reduced five-dimensional Einstein-Gauss-Bonnet gravity. The reduced theory contains gravity, electromagnetism and a scalar field, with nonlinear corrections to the action and nontrivial couplings. The solutions can be classified through mass and electric and magnetic charge. They present peculiar features with respect to the solutions of the standard Kaluza-Klein theory without Gauss-Bonnet corrections, like the existence of an extremal mass even in the neutral case. Also the thermodynamics is affected, for example, extremal black holes display nonvanishing temperature and entropy.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates black hole solutions in dimensionally reduced 5D Einstein-Gauss-Bonnet gravity, yielding a theory with gravity, electromagnetism, and a scalar field featuring nonlinear corrections and nontrivial couplings; it identifies novel features—including extremal mass for neutral black holes and nonzero temperature/entropy for extremal black holes—distinct from standard Kaluza-Klein theory.
AI.motivation: To explore how Gauss-Bonnet curvature corrections modify black hole solutions and thermodynamics in dimensionally reduced five-dimensional gravity, especially relative to standard Kaluza-Klein theory.
AI.method: Dimensional reduction of five-dimensional Einstein-Gauss-Bonnet gravity to obtain an effective four-dimensional theory containing gravity, electromagnetism, and a scalar field; analysis and classification of black hole solutions by mass and electric/magnetic charges; study of thermodynamic properties including temperature and entropy.
AI.result: Discovery of black hole solutions exhibiting an extremal mass even in the absence of electric or magnetic charge; extremal black holes possess nonzero Hawking temperature and finite entropy—violating standard expectations from classical general relativity and Kaluza-Klein theory.
AI.conclusion: Gauss-Bonnet corrections fundamentally alter black hole structure and thermodynamics in reduced dimensions, introducing qualitatively new phenomena such as charge-independent extremality and nonvanishing thermodynamic quantities for extremal configurations.

title: Radiative corrections to the nucleon isovector $g_V$ and $g_A$
summary: Electroweak, QCD, and QED radiative corrections to the nucleon low-energy coupling constants $g_V$ and $g_A$ are enhanced by large perturbative logarithms between the electroweak and hadronic scale, as well as between the hadronic scale and the low-energy MeV scale. Additionally, higher-order pion-mass splitting corrections to the nucleon axial-vector charge might be large. By consistently incorporating these effects, we provide an updated relation between the lattice-QCD and physical $g_A$, finding a total radiative correction of $3.5(2.1)\%$ ($5.6(7)\%$). This leads to an expected lattice-QCD result of $g^{\mathrm{QCD}}_A = 1.265(26)$ ($g^{\mathrm{QCD}}_A = 1.240(9)$) when based on a combination of lattice-QCD and data-driven (or only data-driven) inputs, respectively. Future phenomenological, chiral perturbation theory, and lattice-QCD studies can improve both the central value and the uncertainty of this estimate.

AI.tldr: This paper calculates updated radiative corrections to the nucleon axial-vector coupling $g_A$ and vector coupling $g_V$, accounting for electroweak, QCD, and QED effects plus pion-mass splitting; it yields refined lattice-to-physical $g_A$ conversion factors with ~3.5–5.6% total correction.
AI.motivation: Large perturbative logarithms between electroweak (~100 GeV), hadronic (~1 GeV), and low-energy (~MeV) scales, along with higher-order pion-mass splitting effects, significantly enhance radiative corrections to $g_V$ and $g_A$—necessitating a consistent resummation for precise lattice-QCD to physical-value matching.
AI.method: Systematic inclusion of electroweak, QCD, and QED radiative corrections using renormalization-group evolution across multiple scales, combined with chiral perturbation theory treatment of pion-mass splitting effects on the axial-vector charge.
AI.result: Total radiative correction to $g_A$ is $3.5(2.1)\%$ (lattice + data-driven inputs) or $5.6(7)\%$ (data-driven only); inferred physical $g_A$ implies lattice-QCD predictions of $g^\mathrm{QCD}_A = 1.265(26)$ or $1.240(9)$, respectively.
AI.conclusion: The updated relation improves the precision and reliability of extracting the physical $g_A$ from lattice-QCD simulations; remaining uncertainties call for improved phenomenological input, higher-order chiral calculations, and high-statistics lattice studies.

title: Local Robustness of Bound States in the Continuum through Scattering-Matrix Eigenvector Continuation
summary: We consider the diffraction of time-harmonic plane waves by a periodic structure, governed by the Helmholtz equation. Bound states in the continuum (BICs) are quasi-periodic fields that remain $L^{2}$-bounded over one period and occur at frequencies embedded in the continuous spectrum. Perturbations that break a BIC can lead to ultra-strong resonances, enabling various applications in photonics. Employing the implicit function theorem, we demonstrate how a simple BIC continuously deforms into a propagating field as system parameters vary in a neighborhood, with the frequency adjusting accordingly. In this setting, the incident coefficients of the field persist as an eigenvector of the scattering matrix with a fixed eigenvalue. By introducing a mapping $\mathcal{P}$ from the parameters to these coefficients, the zeros of $\mathcal{P}$ correspond precisely to BICs. When such a zero is isolated and the dimensions of the domain and range coincide, the BIC can be related to the mapping degree of $\mathcal{P}$ in a small neighborhood. This perspective clarifies the phase singularity associated with BICs and provides a general topological interpretation of their local robustness with respect to the given parameters. Moreover, it yields a practical numerical criterion for detecting and verifying BICs via computation of the mapping degree of $\mathcal{P}$.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes bound states in the continuum (BICs) in periodic photonic structures governed by the Helmholtz equation, using the implicit function theorem and a scattering-matrix-based mapping 𝒫 to establish a topological characterization—linking isolated BICs to the mapping degree of 𝒫—and provides a numerical criterion for their detection.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous topological framework for understanding the robustness and phase singularities of BICs in periodic wave systems, and to enable reliable numerical detection beyond perturbative or symmetry-based methods.
AI.method: Applies the implicit function theorem to track BIC deformation under parameter variation; defines a mapping 𝒫 from structural parameters to incident scattering coefficients; identifies BICs as isolated zeros of 𝒫; uses mapping degree theory when domain and codomain dimensions match.
AI.result: Establishes that isolated BICs correspond to non-zero mapping degree of 𝒫, revealing their topological origin and local robustness; derives a numerically verifiable criterion based on computing the degree of 𝒫.
AI.conclusion: BICs possess intrinsic topological structure encoded in the mapping degree of 𝒫, offering both conceptual insight into their stability and a practical, general-purpose computational method for their identification in periodic Helmholtz systems.

title: Schwinger effect in QCD and nuclear physics
comment: 26 pages, 3 figures; a contribution to the Encyclopedia of Nuclear Physics
summary: We provide a pedagogical review of the Schwinger effect, i.e., the non-perturbative production of particle and anti-particle pairs from the vacuum by strong fields, as well as related strong-field phenomena. Beginning with an overview of the Schwinger effect in quantum electrodynamics, we discuss its extensions to quantum chromodynamics and its applications in nuclear physics, including high-$Z$ nuclei, string breaking, relativistic heavy-ion collisions, and the chiral anomaly.

AI.tldr: This paper offers a pedagogical review of the Schwinger effect—the non-perturbative creation of particle–antiparticle pairs from vacuum under strong electromagnetic (and later, chromodynamic) fields—and surveys its theoretical extensions and applications in nuclear physics.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified, accessible pedagogical framework for understanding the Schwinger effect and its broad implications across quantum field theory and nuclear physics, especially where non-perturbative dynamics dominate.
AI.method: Pedagogical review: synthesizes foundational derivations (e.g., instanton/WKB methods in QED), extends conceptual and formal analogies to QCD, and connects to phenomenological contexts (e.g., string breaking, chiral anomaly, heavy-ion collisions).
AI.result: Clarifies the universality of vacuum pair production beyond QED; establishes conceptual bridges between Schwinger-like mechanisms and key nuclear/QCD phenomena—including suppression/enhancement due to confinement, topology (chiral anomaly), and finite-density/temperature effects.
AI.conclusion: The Schwinger mechanism is not only a hallmark of strong-field QED but also a critical conceptual and quantitative tool for probing non-perturbative dynamics in QCD and relativistic nuclear physics.

title: Periodic Orbits and Gravitational Radiation from Extreme Mass-Ratio Inspirals as Probes of Black Hole Quantum Hair
summary: The classical no-hair theorem states that stationary black holes in general relativity can be completely described by only a small set of global parameters. Within this framework, no additional geometric structures are expected to persist outside the event horizon. However, quantum vacuum polarization may introduce small modifications to the near-horizon geometry, effectively giving rise to what is known as quantum hair. Such corrections may provide a possible window into the microscopic structure and thermodynamic properties of black holes. In this work, we examine how the quantum hair parameter γ influences the periodic orbital dynamics of test bodies in extreme mass-ratio inspirals (EMRIs) and their associated gravitational-wave emission. We find that γ significantly modifies the characteristic radii and angular momenta of two important circular orbits, namely the marginally bound orbit (MBO) and the innermost stable circular orbit (ISCO), leading to a shift in the allowed region of the energy-angular momentum (E-L) phase space. Based on the rational number q classification, we further show that quantum corrections tend to enhance the zoom-whirl orbital behavior.Gravitational-wave calculations using the Numerical Kludge approach indicate that quantum hair alters the effective spacetime potential, produces small drifts in the fundamental orbital frequencies, and consequently leads to observable phase dephasing in longduration signals. These results provide a dynamical signature for distinguishing quantum-corrected black holes from classical Schwarzschild ones and offer theoretical motivation for testing quantum gravity effects with future space-based gravitational-wave observatories.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how quantum hair—a correction to black hole geometry from vacuum polarization—affects orbital dynamics and gravitational-wave emission in extreme mass-ratio inspirals (EMRIs), finding measurable phase dephasing and enhanced zoom-whirl behavior that could distinguish quantum-corrected black holes from classical ones.
AI.motivation: To identify observable dynamical signatures of quantum gravity effects—specifically quantum hair arising from vacuum polarization—that could be probed via gravitational waves from EMRIs.
AI.method: Analytical and numerical analysis of circular orbits (MBO and ISCO) under a quantum-corrected spacetime metric parameterized by γ; orbital classification using rational number q; gravitational-wave modeling via the Numerical Kludge approach to compute frequency drifts and phase evolution.
AI.result: Quantum hair parameter γ shifts MBO and ISCO radii and angular momenta, alters the E-L phase space, enhances zoom-whirl behavior, modifies the effective potential, induces orbital frequency drifts, and causes cumulative phase dephasing in long-duration gravitational-wave signals.
AI.conclusion: Quantum hair produces distinct, potentially detectable imprints on EMRI dynamics and waveforms, providing a concrete pathway to test quantum-gravity-motivated black hole structure with future space-based gravitational-wave observatories like LISA.

title: Boundary critical behavior of the Gross-Neveu-Yukawa model
summary: We study the critical behavior of the semi-infinite Gross-Neveu-Yukawa model, a quantum field theory describing Dirac fermions interacting with bosonic fields via a Yukawa coupling. We consider Neumann and Dirichlet boundary conditions for the bosonic fields, and the most general boundary conditions for the fermions that preserve unitarity, conformal invariance, and charge conjugation symmetry. We analyze the phase diagram and identify distinct fixed points corresponding to different universality classes of boundary critical behavior. The associated boundary critical exponents, which govern the scaling behavior and crossover phenomena, are computed to one-loop order. We also discuss the relevance of our results to the semi-infinite pseudoscalar Yukawa model.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes boundary critical behavior in the semi-infinite Gross-Neveu-Yukawa model with Dirac fermions and bosonic fields, computing one-loop boundary critical exponents for various unitary, conformally invariant, charge-conjugation-symmetric boundary conditions (Neumann/Dirichlet for bosons, general for fermions), and maps results to the pseudoscalar Yukawa model.
AI.motivation: To systematically characterize boundary universality classes and critical exponents in a relativistic, interacting fermion-boson quantum field theory with controlled symmetries and realistic boundary conditions.
AI.method: Perturbative renormalization group analysis at one-loop order in dimensional regularization; classification of admissible boundary conditions respecting unitarity, conformal invariance, and charge conjugation symmetry; fixed-point analysis of the boundary-coupled beta functions.
AI.result: Identification of distinct conformal boundary fixed points; computation of boundary critical exponents (e.g., surface anomalous dimensions, crossover exponents) for Neumann and Dirichlet bosonic boundaries and symmetric fermionic boundaries; mapping to the semi-infinite pseudoscalar Yukawa model.
AI.conclusion: The semi-infinite Gross-Neveu-Yukawa model exhibits rich boundary critical behavior governed by multiple conformal boundary fixed points, with quantitatively distinct critical exponents determined at one-loop order—providing a benchmark for boundary quantum criticality in Dirac systems.

title: Observables in $\mathrm{U}(1)^n$ Chern-Simons theory
summary: In this article, we will compute the expectation value of observables (which appear as Wilson loops) in $\mathrm{U}(1)^n$ Chern-Simons theory for closed oriented $3$-manifolds. We will show how the various topological sectors of the observable affect the expectation value and confirm that it is a topological invariant. We will also exhibit in this case as well a form of the CS duality introduced in previous works. Finally, to complete the treatment of this theory, we will compute its zero modes and the equations of motion.

AI.tldr: This paper computes expectation values of Wilson loop observables in U(1)^n Chern-Simons theory on closed oriented 3-manifolds, establishes their topological invariance, confirms CS duality, and derives zero modes and equations of motion.
AI.motivation: To rigorously compute observable expectation values in abelian Chern-Simons theory, clarify the role of topological sectors, verify topological invariance, extend CS duality to the U(1)^n case, and fully characterize the classical and quantum structure via zero modes and equations of motion.
AI.method: Path integral quantization of U(1)^n Chern-Simons theory; explicit computation of Wilson loop expectation values using topological classification of flat connections; analysis of connected components of the moduli space (topological sectors); derivation of equations of motion and identification of zero modes from the action's gauge invariance and Hodge decomposition on 3-manifolds.
AI.result: Expectation values of Wilson loops are expressed in terms of linking numbers and holonomies over torsion classes, confirming topological invariance; CS duality manifests as a level-rank-type correspondence between U(1)^n theories with dual coupling matrices; zero modes are identified with harmonic 1-forms modulo integer periods, and equations of motion are flatness conditions dA_i = 0 for each U(1) connection A_i.
AI.conclusion: The expectation values of Wilson loop observables in U(1)^n Chern-Simons theory are topological invariants fully determined by the manifold’s homology and linking form; CS duality holds in this abelian setting; the classical phase space is parametrized by harmonic 1-forms, and quantization respects the topological sector decomposition.

title: Shadows and Polarization Images of a Four-dimensional Gauss-Bonnet Black Hole Irradiated by a Thick Accretion Disk
summary: We adopt a general relativistic ray-tracing approach to study the shadows and polarization images of spherically symmetric Gauss-Bonnet (GB) black holes enveloped by geometrically thick accretion flows. Specifically, we adopt a phenomenological RIAF-like model and an analytical Hou disk model. In the RIAF-like model, increasing the GB coupling parameter $λ$ reduces both the size and brightness of the higher-order image, while increasing $θ$ alters the shape of the higher-order image and obscures the horizon's outline. The main difference between isotropic and anisotropic emission is that the latter produce distortion of the high-order image in the vertical direction, leading to an elliptical morphology. For the Hou disk model, due to specific regions being geometrically thinner with the conical approximation, the high-order images are narrower with the increase in $λ$ than the RIAF model. While increasing $θ$ enhances the brightness of the direct images outside the higher-order images, but hardly changes the size of the higher-order images, which is in sharp contrast to the RIAF model. Meanwhile, the Hou disk produces polarization patterns that trace the brightness configuration and are affected by $λ$ and $θ$, reflecting the intrinsic structure of spacetime. These results illustrate that intensity and polarization in thick-disk models provide probes of GB black holes and near-horizon accretion dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper uses general relativistic ray-tracing to simulate shadows and polarization images of spherically symmetric Gauss-Bonnet black holes with thick accretion flows (RIAF-like and Hou disk models), revealing how the GB coupling parameter λ and viewing angle θ affect image morphology, brightness, and polarization—offering observational probes of modified gravity and near-horizon physics.
AI.motivation: To probe Gauss-Bonnet gravity and near-horizon accretion dynamics via observable signatures—specifically black hole shadows and polarization patterns—in geometrically thick accretion flows.
AI.method: General relativistic ray-tracing in spherically symmetric Gauss-Bonnet spacetime; two phenomenological thick-disk models: (1) an RIAF-like model with isotropic/anisotropic emission, and (2) an analytical Hou disk model incorporating conical thinning; systematic variation of GB coupling parameter λ and observer inclination θ.
AI.result: In the RIAF model: increasing λ shrinks and dims higher-order images; increasing θ distorts their shape and obscures the horizon outline; anisotropic emission induces vertical elliptical distortion. In the Hou model: higher-order images narrow with λ (unlike RIAF); θ enhances direct-image brightness but barely affects higher-order image size; polarization patterns trace intensity structure and encode λ- and θ-dependent spacetime geometry.
AI.conclusion: Intensity and polarization features in thick-disk models serve as sensitive diagnostics for Gauss-Bonnet black hole parameters and accretion geometry, supporting multimodal electromagnetic tests of modified gravity.

title: Emergent fracton strings from covariant bi-form gauge field theory
summary: We present a covariant field-theoretical framework for a rank-4 tensor gauge field theory describing fractonic string-like objects. We show that the most general quadratic, parity-preserving action naturally leads to a Maxwell-like sector, with tensorial analogues of electric and magnetic fields, Maxwell-like equations, a conserved energy-momentum tensor, and a Lorentz-like force. Remarkably, the theory gives rise to fracton-like string excitations purely from symmetry principles: constraints on the motion of these extended objects appear as Gauss-like laws, without being imposed by hand. One of these laws is new and corresponds to a generalised dipole conservation for closed strings, restricting their mobility and defining a novel class of fractonic string-like excitations. Finally, we uncover a connection to linearised area-metric gravity: in a suitable limit, the theory reduces to known covariant fracton models with rank-2 gauge fields, highlighting a deep link between fractonic matter and gravity-like structures. This provides a unified perspective on higher-rank gauge fields, extended excitations, and emergent gravitational features.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a covariant rank-4 tensor gauge field theory for fractonic strings, deriving Maxwell-like dynamics, conserved currents, and Lorentz-like forces from symmetry alone; it reveals new Gauss-type constraints—including a generalized dipole conservation law for closed strings—that intrinsically restrict mobility and define novel fractonic string excitations; furthermore, it establishes a formal connection to linearised area-metric gravity and recovers known rank-2 fracton models in a limiting case.
AI.motivation: To construct a covariant, field-theoretical framework for fractonic string-like excitations grounded in symmetry principles—avoiding ad hoc constraints—and to uncover deeper connections between higher-rank gauge theories, fractonic matter, and emergent gravitational structures.
AI.method: Formulation of the most general quadratic, parity-preserving, covariant action for a rank-4 tensor gauge field; derivation of equations of motion, conserved energy-momentum tensor, and Lorentz-like force; identification and physical interpretation of constraint equations (Gauss-like laws) as emergent mobility restrictions; analysis of the low-energy/limiting behavior to connect with rank-2 fracton models and linearised area-metric gravity.
AI.result: A fully covariant tensor gauge theory yielding Maxwell-analogous dynamics for rank-4 fields; emergence of fractonic string excitations governed by symmetry-derived constraints—not imposed externally; discovery of a new generalized dipole conservation law for closed strings, defining a novel class of immobile fractonic strings; demonstration that the theory reduces to known rank-2 fracton models and embeds linearised area-metric gravity in appropriate limits.
AI.conclusion: Fractonic string behavior can arise intrinsically from covariant higher-rank gauge symmetry without fine-tuned constraints; the theory unifies fractonic physics, extended object dynamics, and emergent gravity-like geometry, suggesting area-metric structures as a natural geometric language for certain fractonic phases.

title: Low order maximally single-trace graphs as the first counterexamples to large N factorization in random tensors
summary: We give the first and lowest order examples of 3-regular 3-edge-colored graphs that demonstrate the non-factorization of tensor model invariants in the large N limit of Gaussian random tensors, as proven on general grounds in [Gurau R., Joos F. and Sudakov B., Lett. Math. Phys., 115 (2025), arXiv:2506.15362 [math-ph]]. This non-factorization is in stark contrast to the well-known large N factorization for random matrices.

AI.tldr: This paper provides the first explicit examples of 3-regular 3-edge-colored graphs showing that tensor model invariants do not factorize in the large N limit—unlike matrix models—confirming a recent general proof.
AI.motivation: To construct concrete, lowest-order counterexamples demonstrating the breakdown of large N factorization for Gaussian random tensor invariants, contrasting with the established factorization in random matrix theory.
AI.method: Construction and analysis of 3-regular 3-edge-colored graphs—the combinatorial objects encoding tensor invariants—to exhibit non-factorizing contributions in the large N expansion.
AI.result: Explicit 3-regular 3-edge-colored graphs are identified whose associated tensor invariants violate large N factorization, realizing the theoretically predicted non-factorization at the simplest possible order.
AI.conclusion: The existence of such low-order graphs confirms that large N factorization fails generically for tensor models, highlighting a fundamental structural distinction from matrix models.

title: Rephasing invariant structure of Dirac CP phase and basis independent reduction of unitarity constraints for mixing matrices
summary: In this paper, we explore rephasing invariant structures of the Dirac CP phase $δ$ under an approximation $U^{e}_{13} = 0$, where the 1-3 element of the diagonalization of charged leptons $U^{e}$ is neglected. With the further simplified condition $U^{e}_{12} = 0$, the Dirac phase reduces to a compact form $δ= δ^ν + \arg [ (U_{33}^e / U_{23}^{e}) - ( U_{33}^ν / U_{23}^ν) ] - \arg [ ( U_{33}^e / U_{23}^{e}) + (U_{23}^{ν*} / U_{33}^{ν*} ) ] $, and the CP phase for finite $U_{12}^{e}$ can be understood as a generalization of this compact form. These results encompass almost all perturbative calculations of the CP phases in quark and lepton mixing matrices with hierarchical masses of charged fermions, and are independent of any specific parametrization. As a second result of this work, we derive a basis independent reduction of the unitarity constraints for an arbitrary unitary matrix $V$ by eliminating the elements $V_{21}, V_{22}, V_{31}, V_{32}$ using the inversion formula. Applying the explicit rephasing transformation to this reduction yields a rephasing invariant representation of the PDG parametrization, which allows the translation of theoretical results expressed in the PDG parametrization directly into rephasing invariants.
